questions.question: A Snowflake account's monthly storage bill is unexpectedly high. After checking ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS, a team finds TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES is 3x larger than ACTIVE_BYTES for several large tables. What is the likely cause and fix?
options: Queries are scanning too many micro-partitions; add clustering keys to reduce storage

These tables have DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS set to a high value (e.g., 90 days) with heavy DML creating large historical snapshots; reduce retention or change to Transient tables for non-critical data
The tables are incorrectly configured as external tables; convert them to internal tables
Snowflake Fail-Safe is consuming the space; disable Fail-Safe on affected tables
questions.explanation: High TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES relative to ACTIVE_BYTES indicates tables with long Time Travel retention and frequent DML. Every UPDATE/DELETE creates new micro-partitions while old ones are retained for Time Travel. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS or switching non-critical tables to Transient (no Fail-Safe) reduces this overhead.

questions.question: What is the recommended Snowflake file size range for optimal bulk loading performance with COPY INTO?
options: Under 1 MB per file for fastest upload
100 MB to 250 MB per file — large enough for efficient loading but small enough to parallelize across nodes
Over 1 GB per file — fewer, larger files reduce overhead
Exactly 512 MB — Snowflake's micro-partition size boundary
questions.explanation: Snowflake recommends staging files between 100 MB and 250 MB (compressed) for optimal COPY INTO performance. Files should be pre-split into this range to allow parallel loading across warehouse compute nodes. Very small files create excessive metadata overhead; very large files cannot be split across nodes.

questions.question: A COPY INTO command completes but some rows were rejected due to data type errors. How can you find which rows were rejected and what errors occurred?
options: Check the COPY_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE
Query the SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOAD_HISTORY view for error details
Run COPY INTO ... VALIDATION_MODE = 'RETURN_ERRORS' on the same file, or check SYSTEM$COPY_STATUS after the load
Rejected rows are automatically written to a quarantine table with an _ERRORS suffix
questions.explanation: After a COPY INTO with ON_ERROR = CONTINUE, you can use VALIDATE(table, job_id => '<load_job_id>') or COPY INTO with VALIDATION_MODE = 'RETURN_ERRORS' to see rejected rows. SYSTEM$COPY_STATUS and COPY_HISTORY in INFORMATION_SCHEMA also provide load status and error counts per file.

questions.question: Which COPY INTO option makes Snowflake reload files that were already successfully loaded into the target table?
options: RELOAD = TRUE
FORCE = TRUE
OVERWRITE = TRUE
ALLOW_DUPLICATE = TRUE
questions.explanation: FORCE = TRUE in a COPY INTO statement bypasses Snowflake's load deduplication check. Normally, Snowflake tracks loaded files and skips already-loaded ones. FORCE = TRUE causes the files to be reloaded regardless, potentially creating duplicate rows if the table already contains the data.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to load JSON data where the top-level array contains multiple objects (e.g., [{...},{...},{...}]). Which file format option ensures each object becomes a separate row?
options: PARSE_ARRAYS = TRUE
ARRAY_TO_ROWS = TRUE
STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE
ENABLE_JSON_ROWS = TRUE
questions.explanation: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE in a JSON file format definition removes the outermost array brackets and treats each object within the array as a separate row. Without this, the entire top-level array would be loaded as a single VARIANT row.

questions.question: Which Snowflake stage type is automatically created for each user account and is only accessible by that user?
options: Table stage (@%table_name)
User stage (@~)
Named internal stage
User-private stage
questions.explanation: Every Snowflake user has an automatically provisioned user stage, referenced as @~ (tilde). It is personal to that user — only they can upload files to or load from it. It cannot be shared with other users. It is useful for personal data loading tasks.

questions.question: A Snowflake COPY INTO statement loads files from an S3 external stage. The S3 bucket uses server-side encryption with AWS KMS. What must be configured in Snowflake to access these encrypted files?
options: No special configuration needed — Snowflake automatically handles KMS-encrypted S3 files
The Storage Integration must include the KMS key ARN, or the IAM role must have kms:Decrypt permission
A separate encryption integration object must be created in Snowflake
Snowflake cannot access KMS-encrypted S3 files — files must be unencrypted before loading
questions.explanation: When S3 files are encrypted with AWS KMS, the IAM role used by Snowflake's Storage Integration must have the kms:Decrypt permission on the KMS key. This allows Snowflake to decrypt the files as it reads them. No separate Snowflake object is needed — it's handled at the IAM permission level.

questions.question: What does the MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE option do in a COPY INTO statement?
options: Maps CSV columns to table columns by position rather than by name
Maps source file column names to target table column names case-insensitively, useful when column name casing differs between source and target
Ignores columns in the source file that don't match any target table column
Forces all column names to uppercase before matching
questions.explanation: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE (or CASE_SENSITIVE) enables column mapping by name from the source file to the target table, ignoring case differences. This is especially useful when loading Parquet, ORC, or Avro files where column names in the file may differ in case from the target table.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Directory Table in Snowflake?
options: A table that stores directory listings of database objects for browsing in Snowsight
A stage feature that exposes file metadata (path, size, last modified, etag) as queryable rows via SQL SELECT
A system table showing all schemas and their parent databases
A mapping table that tracks which stage files have been loaded into which target tables
questions.explanation: Directory Tables are an optional feature on Snowflake stages that expose the files stored in that stage as SQL-queryable rows. You can SELECT file metadata (RELATIVE_PATH, SIZE, LAST_MODIFIED, ETAG, MD5) from the directory table using @stage_name (as a table reference). This enables SQL-based file discovery and pipeline orchestration.

questions.question: A data engineer runs COPY INTO my_table FROM @my_stage and the load succeeds. They run the same command again 1 hour later with new files added to the stage. What happens?
options: All files (previously loaded + new) are reloaded, creating duplicates
Only the new files are loaded — Snowflake tracks which files have been loaded within the last 64 days and skips previously loaded files
The command fails because the target table already has data
All files are skipped because the stage hasn't changed since the last successful load
questions.explanation: Snowflake maintains a load history for each table, tracking which files have been loaded within the last 64 days. When COPY INTO runs again, previously loaded files are automatically skipped — only new files are processed. This prevents accidental duplicate loading without requiring explicit file management.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax to unload a Snowflake query result to a CSV file in an external S3 stage with gzip compression?
options: EXPORT TABLE my_table TO @my_s3_stage FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=CSV COMPRESSION=GZIP);
COPY INTO @my_s3_stage FROM (SELECT * FROM my_table) FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=CSV COMPRESSION=GZIP);
SELECT * FROM my_table INTO OUTFILE '@my_s3_stage' FORMAT CSV;
UNLOAD SELECT * FROM my_table TO @my_s3_stage COMPRESSION=GZIP;
questions.explanation: COPY INTO <stage_location> FROM <source> is the correct syntax for unloading data. The source can be a table name or a subquery. File format options including compression are specified in the FILE_FORMAT parameter. EXPORT, SELECT INTO OUTFILE, and UNLOAD are not valid Snowflake SQL.

questions.question: When loading a Parquet file from an external stage, what Snowflake data type should the target table column use to store the Parquet data before further transformation?
options: PARQUET
STRUCTURED
VARIANT
BLOB
questions.explanation: Parquet data loaded into Snowflake is stored as VARIANT (Snowflake's semi-structured data type). Columnar Parquet data is preserved as structured VARIANT values that can be queried using colon and dot notation, or spread into structured columns using FLATTEN and explicit casting.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to test whether a CSV file will load cleanly into a table without actually inserting any rows. Which COPY INTO option achieves this?
options: ON_ERROR = DRY_RUN
VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS
TEST_LOAD = TRUE
SIMULATE = TRUE
questions.explanation: COPY INTO ... VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS performs a complete validation pass of the file against the target table schema and returns any errors that would occur — without loading any data. Other valid values are RETURN_ERRORS (sample) and RETURN_<n>_ROWS (validate first n rows).

questions.question: How does Snowflake handle files loaded with COPY INTO when the target table already contains those rows (not using FORCE = TRUE)?
options: Snowflake checks for row-level duplicates and skips duplicate rows
Snowflake skips already-loaded files based on file name and size metadata, not row content
Snowflake raises an error for any file that would create duplicates
Snowflake merges new rows with existing rows using primary keys
questions.explanation: Snowflake's load deduplication is file-based, not row-based. It tracks loaded files by name, size, and ETag/checksum in the load history (for 64 days). If the same file is submitted again, it is skipped. Snowflake does not inspect row content for duplicates — row-level deduplication requires MERGE.

questions.question: A team needs to load data from an internal Snowflake stage into a table every night. The files arrive at 2 AM and must be loaded by 3 AM. Which approach is simplest?
options: Set up Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST for the internal stage
Create a Snowflake Task with a CRON schedule (0 2 * * *) that runs COPY INTO from the stage
Configure a Dynamic Table with a 1-hour TARGET_LAG pointing to the stage
Use Snowpipe Streaming SDK to push files from the stage
questions.explanation: A scheduled Task with CRON = '0 2 * * *' (2 AM daily) running a COPY INTO statement is the simplest solution for scheduled batch loading from an internal stage. Snowpipe AUTO_INGEST is event-driven (not schedule-based) and works best with external stages where cloud storage events trigger loading.

questions.question: A real-time IoT sensor platform generates 10,000 events per second that must appear in Snowflake with latency under 1 second. Which Snowflake ingestion method is most appropriate?
options: COPY INTO with AUTO_INGEST via S3 Event Notifications
Snowpipe using SQS queue-triggered file loading
Snowpipe Streaming via the Java SDK with row-level inserts
Dynamic Table with TARGET_LAG = '1 second'
questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming uses the Snowflake Ingest SDK to push individual rows directly into Snowflake with sub-second latency. It is designed for high-throughput, low-latency streaming use cases where file-based loading (even Snowpipe) cannot meet latency requirements. Dynamic Tables are for transformations, not ingestion.

questions.question: A Snowflake Stream on table ORDERS has been created. An analyst queries the stream but does not consume it with a DML statement. What happens to the stream's offset?
options: The offset advances to the current timestamp since the query was executed
The offset remains unchanged — only DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) that reference the stream advance the offset
The stream resets to capture all historical changes
The stream temporarily pauses until the query is committed
questions.explanation: A Snowflake Stream's offset only advances when the stream is consumed within a DML transaction — specifically when a DML statement (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) references the stream, and that transaction commits successfully. Simply querying (SELECT from) the stream does not advance the offset.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Standard stream and an Insert-only stream in Snowflake?
options: Standard streams track all DML (insert, update, delete); Insert-only streams only track INSERT operations and are optimized for append-only tables and external tables
Standard streams work on permanent tables; Insert-only streams work on transient tables
Standard streams use more credits per hour than Insert-only streams
Insert-only streams require a separate warehouse; Standard streams use serverless compute
questions.explanation: Standard streams track all DML changes: INSERTs (new rows), UPDATEs (old + new versions), and DELETEs. Insert-only streams only track INSERT operations, making them more efficient for tables where data is never updated or deleted (e.g., event logs, external tables). They have lower overhead for pure-append workloads.

questions.question: A Task is configured with a CRON schedule of '0 6 * * MON-FRI'. When does this task execute?
options: Every 6 hours on weekdays
At 6:00 AM every weekday (Monday through Friday)
Every minute from 6 AM to 6 PM on weekdays
At 6:00 AM on Mondays and Fridays only
questions.explanation: The CRON expression '0 6 * * MON-FRI' means: minute=0, hour=6, day=any, month=any, weekday=MON-FRI. This executes at exactly 6:00 AM (hour 6, minute 0) on every Monday through Friday.

questions.question: A Snowflake Task DAG has root task R, child task C1, and grandchild task C2. R fails due to an error. What happens to C1 and C2?
options: C1 runs but C2 is skipped since C2 depends on C1 succeeding
C1 and C2 both run regardless of R's status — child tasks always execute on schedule
C1 and C2 are both skipped — child tasks only run when their parent completes successfully
C2 runs independently since it doesn't directly depend on R
questions.explanation: In a Snowflake Task DAG, child tasks run ONLY after their immediate parent task completes successfully. If a parent task fails, all downstream children (and their children) are skipped for that run. The failure propagates down the DAG and no downstream work executes.

questions.question: Which Snowflake automation feature allows defining a target state as a SQL query and having Snowflake automatically maintain that state by refreshing data as source tables change?
options: Streams with Tasks (imperative pipeline)
Dynamic Tables (declarative pipeline)
Materialized Views (query-based refresh)
Scheduled COPY INTO (batch loading)
questions.explanation: Dynamic Tables let you define a target state as a SQL query (SELECT ... FROM ...) and specify a TARGET_LAG. Snowflake automatically determines what changed in the source and refreshes the Dynamic Table to maintain the target state within the specified lag. This declarative model contrasts with Streams+Tasks which requires imperative code.

questions.question: A Snowpipe is configured with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE pointing to an S3 external stage. Files arrive in S3 but are not being loaded. What is the most likely cause?
options: AUTO_INGEST requires Enterprise edition to function
The SQS notification queue that connects S3 event notifications to Snowpipe is not configured or has stopped delivering messages
Snowpipe only works with internal stages — external stages require manual COPY INTO
The Snowpipe's COPY INTO statement references the wrong table name
questions.explanation: AUTO_INGEST = TRUE relies on cloud storage event notifications delivered via an SQS queue (for S3) or Azure Event Grid / GCP Pub/Sub. If the SQS queue is misconfigured, not set up, has stalled, or the S3 bucket events are not triggering notifications, files will not be loaded automatically.

questions.question: What is a 'stale' Snowflake stream and what causes it?
options: A stream becomes stale when it references a table that has been dropped
A stream becomes stale when it has not been consumed for longer than its staleness threshold (tied to the source table's Time Travel retention period)
A stream becomes stale when the source table has had no DML activity for 30 days
A stream becomes stale after exactly 14 days regardless of consumption or table configuration
questions.explanation: A stream becomes STALE when the data it needs to track changes has been purged from the source table's Time Travel history. The staleness threshold depends on the source table's DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS (default 1 day for Standard edition). A stale stream can no longer accurately track changes and must be recreated.

questions.question: Which Snowflake object allows scheduled SQL execution AND conditional execution based on whether a stream has data?
options: Dynamic Table with conditional refresh
Snowflake Alert with stream condition
Snowflake Task with a WHEN SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() clause
Snowpipe with conditional loading
questions.explanation: Snowflake Tasks support a WHEN clause with conditional expressions. WHEN SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('@stream_name') evaluates whether the stream contains unconsumed changes before executing. If the stream is empty, the Task is skipped, saving warehouse compute credits on empty runs.

questions.question: A data pipeline uses a Task DAG with 5 tasks. The root task runs on a virtual warehouse. Which compute option can reduce costs for tasks that run frequently but for short durations?
options: Use the smallest possible warehouse (XS) for all tasks
Configure tasks as SERVERLESS — Snowflake manages compute automatically and bills only for actual execution time
Enable AUTO_SUSPEND = 10 on the task's warehouse to suspend quickly after each run
Use a multi-cluster warehouse so tasks share compute across the DAG
questions.explanation: Serverless Tasks use Snowflake-managed compute that provisions automatically for each task run and is billed per-second based on actual credit consumption. For short, frequent tasks, this can be much cheaper than keeping a virtual warehouse running (with its 1-minute minimum billing) just for task execution.

questions.question: How does Snowpipe differ from a Snowflake Task that runs COPY INTO on a schedule?
options: Snowpipe supports more file formats than Tasks with COPY INTO
Snowpipe is event-driven (triggered when files arrive in cloud storage) and serverless; Tasks are schedule-driven and use a virtual warehouse
Tasks can load from external stages; Snowpipe can only load from internal stages
Snowpipe is only available on Enterprise edition; Tasks are available on all editions
questions.explanation: Snowpipe uses cloud storage event notifications (S3 SQS, Azure Event Grid, GCP Pub/Sub) to trigger loading as soon as files arrive — latency of seconds to minutes. It is serverless (no warehouse needed). Tasks with COPY INTO run on a defined schedule using a virtual warehouse. Both can load from external stages.

questions.question: A Dynamic Table has a complex SQL query with multiple JOINs and aggregations. The source tables have high-frequency DML. What determines how often the Dynamic Table refreshes?
options: Dynamic Tables always refresh every minute regardless of configuration
The TARGET_LAG setting — Snowflake refreshes as frequently as needed to keep the Dynamic Table within the specified lag target
The number of JOINs in the SQL definition — more JOINs mean less frequent refresh
The warehouse size assigned to the Dynamic Table — larger warehouses refresh more frequently
questions.explanation: TARGET_LAG specifies the maximum acceptable age of data in the Dynamic Table. Snowflake's scheduler determines how often to refresh based on the lag target and the rate of source table changes. A TARGET_LAG of '1 minute' means the Dynamic Table data is at most 1 minute behind the source.

questions.question: A data team uses Apache Kafka to stream events and needs them loaded into Snowflake with minimal latency. Which connector should be used?
options: Snowflake Connector for Spark
Snowflake Kafka Connector
Snowflake JDBC driver with custom consumer code
Snowflake Python Connector with streaming inserts
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Connector for Apache Kafka is a Kafka Connect plugin that automatically reads from Kafka topics and loads data into Snowflake tables. It uses Snowpipe under the hood for efficient, low-latency ingestion, and handles offset management and error recovery automatically.

questions.question: What is the primary use case for a Snowflake Storage Integration object?
options: Integrating Snowflake with external SQL databases for federated queries
Establishing a trust relationship that allows Snowflake to access cloud object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) using IAM roles without storing credentials in Snowflake
Connecting Snowflake to streaming platforms like Kafka and Kinesis
Enabling Snowflake accounts to communicate with each other for data sharing
questions.explanation: A Storage Integration creates a cloud-native trust delegation — instead of storing AWS/Azure/GCP credentials in Snowflake stages, the Storage Integration uses IAM role assumption (AWS), Managed Identity (Azure), or Service Account delegation (GCP) to securely access cloud storage. This is the recommended secure approach for external stages.

questions.question: Which Snowflake feature allows calling an external HTTPS API endpoint from within a SQL query?
options: External Stage with HTTP file format
External Function backed by an API Integration
Snowflake Cortex COMPLETE() with a custom URL endpoint
HTTP UDF using Python requests library
questions.explanation: External Functions allow Snowflake SQL to invoke external HTTPS endpoints (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, GCP Cloud Functions via API Gateway). An API Integration object stores the trust relationship between Snowflake and the API Gateway. The external function is called like any Snowflake UDF in SQL.

questions.question: A development team stores Snowflake stored procedures in GitHub. They want to execute these procedures directly from the Git repository without copying code into Snowflake. Which feature enables this?
options: Snowflake Marketplace for code distribution
Git Integration — creates a repository stage backed by a Git repository
External Stage pointing to GitHub raw content URLs
Snowflake CLI with a git pull command before execution
questions.explanation: Snowflake's Git Integration creates a special repository stage that is backed by a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Bitbucket). Stored procedures and UDFs can be executed directly from files in this repository stage, enabling GitOps workflows for Snowflake code management.

questions.question: A Python application uses the Snowflake Python Connector (snowflake-connector-python) to execute a large SELECT query. The result has 5 million rows. Where are these rows processed?
options: Entirely in Snowflake — rows are processed server-side and only a summary is returned
The query executes in Snowflake, but all 5 million rows are fetched to the client application's memory via the connector
Half the rows are processed in Snowflake and half in the client application
The Python connector uses streaming — rows are processed one at a time without storing all in memory
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Python Connector executes queries on Snowflake's infrastructure (using a virtual warehouse), but then fetches result rows to the client application. For 5 million rows, all result data is transferred to the client. Snowpark is the alternative that keeps processing server-side without row-by-row client transfer.

questions.question: When should you use the Snowflake Connector for Apache Spark instead of the Snowflake JDBC driver for Spark?
options: When reading fewer than 100,000 rows — JDBC is better for large datasets
For large-scale Spark-Snowflake ETL, as the Spark Connector uses efficient bulk file transfer rather than row-by-row JDBC, providing significantly better performance
When using Python PySpark — the Spark Connector only supports Scala and Java Spark
When the Spark cluster is on a different cloud provider than the Snowflake account
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Connector for Apache Spark uses bulk file-based data transfer (staging to/from cloud object storage) rather than JDBC's row-by-row protocol. For large datasets typical in Spark workloads, this bulk transfer approach is orders of magnitude faster than JDBC.

questions.question: What does an API Integration object in Snowflake define?
options: The connection parameters for external databases accessed via external stages
The trust relationship and allowed HTTPS endpoint URL prefixes for External Functions — it tells Snowflake which API Gateway endpoint External Functions can call
The authentication credentials for connecting Snowflake to REST APIs
The rate limits and throttling settings for external API calls from Snowflake
questions.explanation: An API Integration object specifies the trust relationship between Snowflake and an external API Gateway (AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management, GCP API Gateway). It defines the allowed endpoint URL prefixes that External Functions can invoke and uses cloud-native IAM trust to authenticate calls to the API.

questions.question: A BI tool uses ODBC to connect to Snowflake. The connection drops after 30 minutes of inactivity. What Snowflake parameter controls this behavior?
options: NETWORK_POLICY_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE — when set to TRUE, prevents session timeout due to inactivity
AUTO_SUSPEND = 1800 on the warehouse
IDLE_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS on the user account
questions.explanation: CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE = TRUE causes the Snowflake client (including ODBC/JDBC connections) to periodically send heartbeats to keep the session alive during inactivity. Without this, sessions time out after a period of inactivity defined by the CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE_HEARTBEAT_FREQUENCY parameter.

questions.question: Which Snowflake driver interface does Excel use to connect to Snowflake for refreshing data connections?
options: JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)
ODBC (Open Database Connectivity)
Python Connector via embedded Python
REST API via Snowflake's SQL API
questions.explanation: Microsoft Excel uses ODBC (Open Database Connectivity) to connect to external databases including Snowflake. Users install the Snowflake ODBC driver on their Windows machine and configure a DSN (Data Source Name) that Excel uses to refresh queries and pivot tables connected to Snowflake.

questions.question: A data engineer creates a Snowflake External Stage that references an S3 bucket in a different AWS account. What is required to allow cross-account S3 access?
options: VPC Peering between the Snowflake AWS VPC and the S3 bucket's AWS account
A Storage Integration with an IAM role in the S3 bucket's account that trusts Snowflake's IAM principal
An AWS Direct Connect link between Snowflake and the external S3 account
Snowflake cannot access S3 buckets in different AWS accounts
questions.explanation: Cross-account S3 access is achieved through IAM role trust. A Storage Integration creates a Snowflake IAM principal (AWS account + external ID). In the target S3 bucket's AWS account, an IAM role with S3 access is created with a trust policy that allows Snowflake's principal to assume it. This is the standard AWS cross-account pattern.

questions.question: In the Snowflake Query Profile, what does 'Bytes Spilled to Local Storage' indicate?
options: Data was written to an external stage during query execution
Query intermediate results exceeded the warehouse's in-memory capacity and were written to the warehouse's local SSD disk
The query result was too large to fit in the query result cache
Data was temporarily moved to Fail-Safe storage during the query
questions.explanation: 'Bytes Spilled to Local Storage' means query processing intermediate data exceeded the warehouse's available memory and spilled to the warehouse's local SSD disk. This is slower than in-memory processing but faster than remote storage spill. The fix is to increase warehouse size for more memory.

questions.question: A Snowflake Query Profile shows an 'Exploding Joins' warning. What does this indicate?
options: The JOIN operation is using too many compute nodes across the warehouse cluster
The JOIN produces significantly more output rows than input rows, often indicating a missing or incorrect join condition causing a near-Cartesian product
The JOIN involves more than 3 tables, which Snowflake flags as potentially inefficient
The JOIN columns are not indexed, causing a full table scan on both sides
questions.explanation: An Exploding Join occurs when a JOIN operation produces output rows that vastly outnumber the input rows from either table. This typically indicates a many-to-many relationship without a proper join key, or a missing join condition that creates a Cartesian product. It is a major performance anti-pattern.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides the most granular detail for analyzing individual query performance over the past year?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY_BY_SESSION
ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY stores one year of detailed per-query execution information including: total elapsed time, compilation time, execution time, bytes scanned, bytes spilled, rows produced, warehouse used, and credits consumed. It is the primary tool for historical query performance analysis.

questions.question: A query reads data from a very large table but the WHERE clause uses a low-selectivity predicate that matches 95% of rows. What performance symptom would you expect in the Query Profile?
options: Very low 'Bytes Scanned' because 95% selectivity means good pruning
Very high 'Bytes Scanned' and close to 100% 'Partitions Scanned' because the predicate doesn't prune much data
The query will use the Search Optimization Service automatically for low-selectivity queries
The query result will be served from the Result Cache
questions.explanation: A low-selectivity predicate (matches 95% of rows) means the query cannot prune many micro-partitions — most partitions contain matching rows. This results in nearly 100% Partitions Scanned and very high Bytes Scanned. Optimization strategies for high-selectivity queries (SOS, clustering) don't help here.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view column tells you how long a query was waiting for a compute slot before it started executing?
options: COMPILATION_TIME
EXECUTION_TIME
QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME
QUEUED_PROVISIONING_TIME
questions.explanation: QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME in ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY records the duration a query spent waiting in the warehouse queue because all compute slots were occupied by other queries. High QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME consistently indicates a need for multi-cluster warehousing or a larger warehouse.

questions.question: What is the 'Inefficient Pruning' indicator in the Snowflake Query Profile?
options: The query reads more micro-partitions than necessary because the data is not well-clustered on the filter column
The query uses an inefficient sort algorithm that reads partitions in the wrong order
The query's WHERE clause uses OR conditions which bypass partition metadata
Inefficient Pruning indicates the warehouse's SSD cache was not used during the scan
questions.explanation: Inefficient Pruning appears when the Query Profile shows a high percentage of micro-partitions scanned despite a WHERE clause that should filter data. This typically means the table's data is not organized (clustered) on the filter column, forcing the scanner to read most partitions even though only a small fraction of data matches.

questions.question: A data engineer notices their nightly ETL queries have high QUEUED_PROVISIONING_TIME in ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY. What does this indicate?
options: The warehouse had too many concurrent queries and queries were waiting for slots
The warehouse was suspended and needed time to provision and warm up before the queries could execute
The Cloud Services layer was unavailable during query compilation
The warehouse was being resized during the ETL run
questions.explanation: QUEUED_PROVISIONING_TIME records the time a query waited while the warehouse was being started (provisioned) from a SUSPENDED state. High QUEUED_PROVISIONING_TIME means the warehouse was frequently suspending and requiring cold-start provisioning. Solutions include increasing AUTO_SUSPEND timeout or keeping the warehouse always warm.

questions.question: Which approach is BEST for identifying the 10 most expensive queries (by execution time) run last week in a Snowflake account?
options: Use EXPLAIN on each query to estimate cost
Query ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD(week,-1,CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) ORDER BY TOTAL_ELAPSED_TIME DESC LIMIT 10
Check the Warehouse Monitoring tab in Snowsight for the top 10 queries
Run SHOW QUERIES to list recent query history
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY retains 1 year of query data with full execution metrics including TOTAL_ELAPSED_TIME. Filtering by START_TIME for the past week and ordering by TOTAL_ELAPSED_TIME DESC LIMIT 10 efficiently identifies the slowest queries. SHOW QUERIES and EXPLAIN don't provide historical analysis.

questions.question: What is 'workload management' in Snowflake and why is it important for performance?
options: Manually scheduling queries to run at specific times to avoid resource contention
Grouping similar workloads onto appropriately sized and configured dedicated warehouses to prevent interference between different query patterns
Monitoring warehouse credit usage to stay under budget
Setting query priorities so critical queries always run before non-critical ones
questions.explanation: Workload management in Snowflake means grouping similar workloads (e.g., ETL, BI, ML, ad-hoc) onto dedicated, appropriately sized warehouses. This prevents interference — a heavy ETL job won't slow down dashboard queries, and vice versa. Each warehouse can be independently sized, scaled, and configured for its workload type.

questions.question: When should you use Search Optimization Service (SOS) vs. a Clustering Key to improve query performance?
options: SOS for low-cardinality range queries; Clustering Keys for high-cardinality equality lookups — they are opposite in use
Clustering Keys for low-to-medium cardinality range/equality filters (DATE, REGION); SOS for high-cardinality equality lookups (CUSTOMER_ID, ORDER_ID)
They are interchangeable — choose whichever is less expensive for your workload
SOS is always better than Clustering Keys because it requires no data reorganization
questions.explanation: Clustering Keys work best on low-to-medium cardinality columns (DATE, REGION, CATEGORY) where organizing micro-partitions by that column allows effective range pruning. SOS is optimized for high-cardinality equality lookups (WHERE customer_id = 12345) where random distribution makes clustering ineffective.

questions.question: A Materialized View is defined on a large fact table that undergoes 100,000 row updates per hour. What is the performance tradeoff?
options: The MV has no tradeoff — it is always faster because results are pre-computed
The MV's background maintenance incurs compute credits for each refresh triggered by source table DML, which may outweigh query performance benefits for very high DML rates
The MV cannot be maintained with more than 1,000 updates per hour — it becomes stale automatically
The MV will block write operations to the source table during refresh
questions.explanation: Materialized Views are automatically refreshed by Snowflake in the background when base table data changes. For high-DML tables (100K updates/hour), the incremental maintenance cost can be significant. If the MV is rarely queried or the base table changes very frequently, the maintenance credits may exceed the query performance benefit.

questions.question: What does SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH return for a Snowflake table?
options: The number of distinct values in the clustering key column
The average number of micro-partitions each unique clustering key value spans — lower values indicate better clustering
The total number of micro-partitions in the table
The compression ratio achieved by the clustering key
questions.explanation: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH returns the average depth of the clustering key — specifically, the average number of micro-partitions that contain each distinct clustering key value. A depth of 1.0 is perfect (each value in exactly 1 partition). Higher depth means more partitions must be scanned per value, indicating degraded clustering.

questions.question: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is enabled on a warehouse. Which type of query benefits MOST from QAS?
options: Simple primary key lookups: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_id = 12345
Large analytical queries with full table scans, aggregations, and complex joins on hundreds of millions of rows
Queries that read only from the warehouse's local SSD cache
Metadata queries (COUNT(*), MIN, MAX) served by Cloud Services
questions.explanation: QAS offloads portions of eligible large-scale queries (full scans, aggregations, joins) to additional Snowflake-managed serverless compute. Simple point lookups are already fast and don't benefit. Metadata queries don't use a warehouse at all. QAS has the highest impact on compute-intensive OLAP queries that overwhelm a single warehouse cluster.

questions.question: A query on a 5 TB table scans only 2% of micro-partitions despite no explicit clustering key. What explains this efficiency?
options: Snowflake automatically creates hidden clustering keys on frequently queried columns
The table was loaded in a natural order that matches the query's filter column, creating natural clustering
The Query Acceleration Service automatically pruned partitions before the scan
The warehouse's SSD cache contained all 2% of needed micro-partitions from previous queries
questions.explanation: When data is loaded in a sequential order (e.g., daily loads sorted by date), micro-partitions naturally contain contiguous value ranges for the ordering column. This 'natural clustering' allows effective partition pruning using only the Cloud Services metadata — no explicit clustering key configuration is needed.

questions.question: What is the correct command to add Search Optimization to a specific column rather than the entire table?
options: ALTER TABLE orders SET SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION = TRUE ON COLUMN customer_id;
ALTER TABLE orders ADD SEARCH OPTIMIZATION ON EQUALITY(customer_id, order_id);
CREATE SEARCH INDEX ON orders(customer_id);
ALTER TABLE orders MODIFY COLUMN customer_id SET SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION;
questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE <table> ADD SEARCH OPTIMIZATION ON EQUALITY(col1, col2) (or SUBSTRING, GEO) allows adding Search Optimization targeted to specific columns and predicate types. This is more cost-efficient than enabling SOS on the entire table, especially for very wide tables with only a few high-cardinality lookup columns.

questions.question: A dashboard runs the same 5 queries every morning for 200 users. The queries are identical SQL but run against data that updated overnight. Will the Result Cache be useful?
options: Yes — the Result Cache stores results for 24 hours regardless of data changes
No — any DML on the underlying tables invalidates the Result Cache for queries touching those tables, so overnight updates will cause cache misses
Partially — the first user hits the cache, subsequent users get live results
Yes — the cache serves the stale result and Snowflake refreshes it asynchronously
questions.explanation: The Query Result Cache is invalidated when DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, COPY INTO) is executed on any of the tables that the cached query depends on. Since data updated overnight causes cache invalidation, the first morning execution of each query will scan actual data. Subsequent identical queries within 24 hours (with no further DML) would hit the cache.

questions.question: A clustering key is defined on (COUNTRY, CITY) for a table. A query filters only on CITY (not COUNTRY). How effective is partition pruning?
options: Very effective — CITY is in the clustering key so full pruning applies
Limited — micro-partitions are primarily organized by COUNTRY, then CITY within each country group. Filtering only on CITY without COUNTRY provides partial pruning at best
No pruning — composite keys require all columns to be specified for pruning
Same effectiveness as filtering on COUNTRY — the key is symmetric
questions.explanation: In a composite clustering key (COUNTRY, CITY), data is primarily sorted by COUNTRY, then by CITY within each COUNTRY grouping. Filtering on COUNTRY alone enables excellent pruning. Filtering on CITY alone provides limited pruning because matching cities may be spread across many COUNTRY micro-partitions.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of the Query Acceleration Service (QAS) compared to simply increasing warehouse size?
options: QAS is cheaper than increasing warehouse size for all query types

QAS offloads eligible portions of a query to a serverless pool, providing additional compute beyond warehouse capacity for specific heavy operations without permanently provisioning a larger warehouse
QAS replaces the warehouse for eligible queries, reducing warehouse credits to zero
QAS adds more parallel nodes permanently to the warehouse cluster
questions.explanation: QAS dynamically offloads eligible portions of queries (large scans, aggregations) to a Snowflake-managed serverless compute pool ON DEMAND. Unlike increasing warehouse size (which costs more per hour all the time), QAS only costs credits when it's actually used for eligible query portions — more cost-efficient for intermittent heavy queries.

questions.question: A user runs SELECT MAX(sale_date) FROM daily_sales. The table has not been modified in the past week. Which cache serves this result?
options: Query Result Cache — because this exact query was run before
Warehouse SSD Cache — because the query scans the sale_date column
Metadata Cache in Cloud Services — MAX() on unmodified tables is served from partition-level statistics
No cache — aggregate queries always require a warehouse to scan data
questions.explanation: Snowflake's Cloud Services layer maintains per-partition statistics including minimum and maximum values for each column. MAX() on an unmodified table can be derived from these statistics without scanning any data or using a warehouse — served directly from the metadata cache, returning instantly.

questions.question: Which action causes the Query Result Cache to be invalidated for a specific query?
options: A different user runs the same query in a different session
The virtual warehouse used for the original query is suspended
Any DML operation (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, COPY INTO) is executed on any table the query references
The 24-hour TTL expires — this is the only invalidation trigger
questions.explanation: The Query Result Cache is invalidated when any of the tables referenced by the cached query have DML applied to them (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, COPY INTO). The 24-hour TTL is a secondary expiration. Different users running the same query do not invalidate each other's caches (though they need the same role context to hit the same cache entry).

questions.question: Two different virtual warehouses run the same large table scan query. Can the second warehouse benefit from the first warehouse's SSD cache?
options: Yes — SSD caches are shared across all warehouses for the same table
No — each virtual warehouse has its own independent SSD cache; warehouse B's cache is completely separate from warehouse A's
Yes — but only if both warehouses have the same size
Only if the warehouses are in the same multi-cluster warehouse group
questions.explanation: Each Snowflake virtual warehouse has its own independent local SSD disk cache. Warehouse B cannot access or benefit from data cached in Warehouse A's SSD. Each warehouse must populate its own SSD cache by scanning data from cloud object storage on its first access. The Query Result Cache in Cloud Services IS shared across warehouses.

questions.question: A query is run by user A with role ANALYST, yielding a result from the Result Cache. User B with role DEVELOPER runs the identical SQL. Will user B get the cached result?
options: Yes — the Result Cache is keyed only on SQL text, not on user or role
No — the Result Cache key includes the active role context; DEVELOPER and ANALYST are different roles, so each gets a separate cache entry
No — the Result Cache only serves the user who originally ran the query
Yes — if the table hasn't changed, any user gets the cached result
questions.explanation: The Query Result Cache key includes the SQL text, database, schema, warehouse, and active role. Since ANALYST and DEVELOPER are different roles (which may have different row/column access policies), the same query from DEVELOPER results in a separate cache lookup from ANALYST's cached result.

questions.question: A warehouse runs queries in the morning and auto-suspends at noon. New queries arrive at 6 PM. What is the state of the SSD cache when the 6 PM queries run?
options: The SSD cache is fully intact from the morning — suspension preserves the cache
The SSD cache is empty — suspension clears all cached data blocks, requiring a cold start
The SSD cache contains only metadata — data blocks are purged but statistics remain
The SSD cache is available for read but new data cannot be added until it fully warms up
questions.explanation: When a Snowflake virtual warehouse suspends (manually or via AUTO_SUSPEND), its local SSD cache is completely cleared. When the warehouse resumes for the 6 PM queries, it starts with an empty cache. Data blocks must be re-read from cloud object storage on the first queries — this is called a 'cold start'.

questions.question: Which SYSTEM function can be used in a Snowflake query to check whether the result was served from the Result Cache?
options: SYSTEM$RESULT_CACHE_HIT()
SYSTEM$QUERY_CACHE_STATUS()
SELECT ... FROM ... SHOW RESULT_CACHE
Query the QUERY_HISTORY view and check the IS_CLIENT_GENERATED_STATEMENT or EXECUTION_STATUS columns
questions.explanation: To determine if a query was served from the Result Cache, check ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY or INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY. Look at the QUERY_ACCELERATION_BYTES_SCANNED or check if EXECUTION_TIME is extremely low (milliseconds) suggesting cache hit. In Snowsight, the Query Profile shows 'No Query Profile (Result Cache Hit)' for cached results.

questions.question: Which SQL statement correctly accesses the 'price' field nested inside a 'product' object inside a VARIANT column named 'order_data'?
options: order_data.product.price
order_data:product.price::FLOAT
order_data['product']['price']
Both B and C are valid
questions.explanation: Both the colon-dot notation (order_data:product.price::FLOAT) and bracket notation (order_data['product']['price']) are valid ways to navigate nested VARIANT fields in Snowflake. The cast (::FLOAT) is recommended to ensure the value is interpreted correctly. Dot notation alone (A) is not the standard Snowflake VARIANT syntax.

questions.question: What is the result of the FLATTEN function when applied to an empty array in a VARIANT column?
options: It returns one row with NULL values
It returns zero rows — FLATTEN on an empty array produces no output rows
It raises an error — FLATTEN cannot process empty arrays
It returns one row with an empty VARIANT value
questions.explanation: FLATTEN on an empty array [] produces zero output rows. Since there are no elements to expand, the LATERAL JOIN with FLATTEN returns no rows for that input record. This is important to understand when LEFT JOINing with FLATTEN — use OUTER => TRUE if you want to preserve records with empty arrays.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to calculate the cumulative sum of sales for each product, ordered by date, resetting the sum for each product. Which SQL pattern achieves this?
options: SELECT product_id, sale_date, SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS cumulative_sales FROM sales;
SELECT product_id, sale_date, SUM(amount) GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY sale_date AS cumulative_sales FROM sales;
SELECT product_id, sale_date, CUMSUM(amount) PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY sale_date FROM sales;
SELECT product_id, sale_date, RUNNING_SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY sale_date) FROM sales;
questions.explanation: SUM() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ... ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) is the standard SQL window function for cumulative running totals. PARTITION BY resets the sum for each product. ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW includes all rows from the partition start up to the current row.

questions.question: Which SQL function returns the value from the row immediately following the current row within the window partition?
options: NEXT()
LEAD(col, 1)
LAG(col, -1)
OFFSET_VALUE(col, 1)
questions.explanation: LEAD(col, offset, default) returns the value of a column from a row that is 'offset' rows ahead of the current row within the window partition. LEAD(col, 1) returns the next row's value. LAG() looks backwards (previous rows). NEXT() and OFFSET_VALUE() are not Snowflake SQL functions.

questions.question: A developer uses GROUPING SETS in SQL. What does GROUPING SETS ((a, b), (a), ()) compute?
options: A CUBE of columns a and b
Subtotals grouped by (a, b) together, by (a) alone, and a grand total () — equivalent to ROLLUP(a, b) in this case
Only the grand total in ()
All combinations of a and b including (b) alone — equivalent to CUBE
questions.explanation: GROUPING SETS ((a, b), (a), ()) explicitly specifies which grouping combinations to compute: (1) grouped by both a and b, (2) grouped by a only, (3) grand total (no grouping). This is equivalent to ROLLUP(a, b) which generates the same three groupings hierarchically. CUBE(a, b) would also include (b) alone.

questions.question: What is the difference between RANK() and ROW_NUMBER() in Snowflake window functions?
options: RANK() and ROW_NUMBER() are identical for all data including ties
ROW_NUMBER() assigns a unique sequential number to every row (no ties); RANK() assigns the same rank to tied rows and skips subsequent rank values (e.g., 1,1,3,4)
RANK() is faster because it doesn't evaluate tie conditions
ROW_NUMBER() is non-deterministic for ties; RANK() always produces consistent results
questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() always assigns a unique integer to each row — for tied values, the assignment is arbitrary but always unique (e.g., 1,2,3,4). RANK() assigns the same rank to tied values and skips the intermediate rank positions (e.g., 1,1,3,4). DENSE_RANK() handles ties like RANK() but without gaps (e.g., 1,1,2,3).

questions.question: A Snowflake VARIANT column contains a JSON array of strings: ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']. Which query correctly returns each fruit as a separate row?
options: SELECT value::STRING FROM my_table, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => fruits_col) WHERE fruits_col IS NOT NULL
SELECT SPLIT(fruits_col, ',') FROM my_table
SELECT UNNEST(fruits_col) FROM my_table
SELECT EXPLODE(fruits_col) FROM my_table
questions.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => <variant_array_col>) expands a VARIANT array into individual rows. The VALUE column in the FLATTEN output contains each array element. Casting with ::STRING converts the VARIANT value to a string. SPLIT, UNNEST, and EXPLODE are not the correct Snowflake syntax for VARIANT array expansion.

questions.question: A developer uses PARSE_JSON to process a column where some rows contain invalid JSON. Which function prevents the query from failing on invalid JSON?
options: SAFE_PARSE_JSON()
TRY_PARSE_JSON()
VALIDATE_JSON()
PARSE_JSON() with ON_ERROR = NULL
questions.explanation: TRY_PARSE_JSON() is the error-safe version of PARSE_JSON(). If the input is not valid JSON, TRY_PARSE_JSON() returns NULL instead of raising an error. This is part of Snowflake's TRY_ prefix family of functions that return NULL on conversion failure rather than throwing exceptions.

questions.question: What does SELECT OBJECT_CONSTRUCT('name', 'Alice', 'age', 30) return in Snowflake?
options: A STRING containing the JSON text '{"name":"Alice","age":30}'
A VARIANT object {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30} that can be queried with colon notation
A TABLE with columns 'name' and 'age'
An error — OBJECT_CONSTRUCT only accepts column references, not literals
questions.explanation: OBJECT_CONSTRUCT(key1, val1, key2, val2, ...) builds a Snowflake VARIANT object (equivalent to a JSON object) from key-value pairs. The result is a VARIANT value that can be queried with colon notation, stored in a VARIANT column, or used in further JSON construction.

questions.question: A data transformation pipeline needs to convert a table's CATEGORY column into separate boolean columns (one-hot encoding). Which SQL feature transforms one value per row into multiple columns?
options: UNPIVOT — converts rows to columns
PIVOT — rotates row values into column headers with aggregated values
GROUPING SETS — creates multiple aggregation levels
TRANSPOSE — generic row/column swap function
questions.explanation: PIVOT rotates distinct values from a column into separate columns, with an aggregate function applied. For one-hot encoding, PIVOT(COUNT(*) FOR category IN ('A', 'B', 'C')) creates columns A, B, C with 1 or 0 values per row. UNPIVOT does the reverse (columns to rows). TRANSPOSE is not a Snowflake function.

questions.question: What is the correct SQL to calculate a 7-day rolling average of daily_sales for each store?
options: SELECT store_id, sale_date, AVG(daily_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM sales
SELECT store_id, sale_date, AVG(daily_sales) GROUP BY store_id, ROLLING(sale_date, 7) FROM sales
SELECT store_id, sale_date, ROLLING_AVG(daily_sales, 7) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY sale_date) FROM sales
SELECT store_id, AVG(daily_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY store_id ORDER BY sale_date RANGE BETWEEN 7 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM sales
questions.explanation: A 7-day rolling average uses ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW (6 previous rows + current row = 7 rows total). PARTITION BY store_id calculates separately for each store. ROLLING_AVG() and ROLLING() are not standard Snowflake SQL functions. RANGE BETWEEN uses value-based ranges, not row-count-based.

questions.question: A developer needs the first and last values of a column within each window partition. Which SQL functions return these values?
options: MIN() and MAX() with ORDER BY in the OVER clause
FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE() window functions
HEAD() and TAIL() window functions
ROW_NUMBER() = 1 and ROW_NUMBER() = COUNT(*)
questions.explanation: FIRST_VALUE(col) and LAST_VALUE(col) are Snowflake window functions that return the first and last values of a column within the window frame. Note: LAST_VALUE() behavior depends on the frame definition — the default frame (RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) may not include all rows for LAST_VALUE without explicitly setting ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING.

questions.question: Which Snowflake function converts a VARIANT value to a formatted JSON string?
options: TO_JSON()
VARIANT_TO_JSON()
TO_CHAR(variant_col, 'JSON')
AS_VARCHAR(variant_col)
questions.explanation: TO_JSON(variant_value) converts a VARIANT value (object, array, or scalar) to its JSON string representation. This is the inverse of PARSE_JSON(). It's useful for exporting VARIANT data as JSON strings for external consumption or for comparing VARIANT content as text.

questions.question: A developer wants to check if a VARIANT value represents a JSON array (as opposed to an object or scalar). Which function is used?
options: TYPEOF(variant_val) = 'ARRAY'
IS_ARRAY(variant_val)
CHECK_TYPE(variant_val, 'array')
variant_val::ARRAY IS NOT NULL
questions.explanation: IS_ARRAY(variant_val) returns TRUE if the VARIANT value is a JSON array. Snowflake provides similar type-checking functions: IS_OBJECT(), IS_ARRAY(), IS_VARCHAR(), IS_INTEGER(), IS_DECIMAL(), IS_BOOLEAN(), IS_NULL_VALUE(). TYPEOF() returns a string type name but the correct comparison would be TYPEOF(v) = 'array'.

questions.question: What does applying LATERAL FLATTEN with OUTER => TRUE do compared to regular FLATTEN?
options: OUTER => TRUE makes FLATTEN process nested arrays inside arrays (multi-level flattening)
OUTER => TRUE preserves source rows even when the array is NULL or empty — a LEFT JOIN equivalent
OUTER => TRUE returns the outer (top-level) keys only, not nested values
OUTER => TRUE enables recursive processing of deeply nested JSON structures
questions.explanation: By default, FLATTEN performs an inner join behavior — source rows with NULL or empty arrays produce no output rows. With OUTER => TRUE, FLATTEN behaves like a LEFT OUTER JOIN: source rows with NULL or empty arrays produce a single output row with NULL values in the FLATTEN columns, preserving the source row in the output.

questions.question: A developer writes: SELECT NTH_VALUE(salary, 3) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) FROM employees. What does this return?
options: The 3rd highest salary in each department
The salary of the 3rd employee when ordered by descending salary within each department
The average of the top 3 salaries per department
The 3rd row's salary in the original table order
questions.explanation: NTH_VALUE(col, n) returns the value of 'col' from the n-th row of the window frame. With ORDER BY salary DESC, the 3rd row has the 3rd highest salary in each department partition. Note: the window frame definition affects which 'n-th' row is returned — ensure the frame includes all rows (ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) for consistent results.

questions.question: Which Snowflake function produces a JSON array from multiple rows of a column?
options: COLLECT_LIST(col)
ARRAY_AGG(col)
GROUP_CONCAT(col)
JSON_ARRAYAGG(col)
questions.explanation: ARRAY_AGG(col) is the Snowflake aggregate function that collects values from multiple rows into a single VARIANT array. It is the Snowflake equivalent of GROUP_CONCAT (MySQL) or COLLECT_LIST (Spark). The result is a VARIANT value containing a JSON array of the collected values.

questions.question: A query needs to count the number of rows where a specific condition is true, within a GROUP BY aggregation. Which approach is most efficient in Snowflake?
options: Use a subquery: SELECT dept, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.dept = e1.dept AND e2.salary > 100000) FROM employees e1 GROUP BY dept
Use COUNT with a CASE expression: SELECT dept, COUNT(CASE WHEN salary > 100000 THEN 1 END) AS high_earners FROM employees GROUP BY dept
Use SUM with a boolean cast: SELECT dept, SUM(CASE WHEN salary > 100000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS high_earners FROM employees GROUP BY dept
Both B and C are correct and efficient
questions.explanation: Both COUNT(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 END) and SUM(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) are efficient single-pass aggregation patterns that count conditional rows within GROUP BY. COUNT ignores NULLs (so THEN 1 without ELSE works), while SUM requires explicit 0/1. Both avoid correlated subqueries which would be less efficient.

questions.question: What is the QUALIFY clause in Snowflake SQL used for?
options: Replacing a HAVING clause for non-aggregate filters
Filtering rows based on window function results without requiring a subquery or CTE
Specifying the time zone for timestamp operations
Qualifying column names to avoid ambiguity in self-joins
questions.explanation: QUALIFY is a Snowflake-specific SQL extension that filters rows based on window function results directly in the main query, without needing a subquery or CTE. For example: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) rn FROM employees QUALIFY rn = 1 — this returns only the highest-paid employee per department.

questions.question: A developer wants to restore a table to the state it was in before a specific MERGE statement corrupted data. The query ID of the MERGE is known. Which Time Travel syntax is most appropriate?
options: SELECT * FROM orders AT (OFFSET => -3600)
CREATE TABLE orders_restore AS SELECT * FROM orders BEFORE (STATEMENT => '<merge_query_id>')
SELECT * FROM orders AT (TIMESTAMP => CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() - INTERVAL '1 HOUR')
UNDROP TABLE orders
questions.explanation: BEFORE (STATEMENT => '<query_id>') retrieves the table state as it existed immediately BEFORE the specified statement executed. This is the most precise Time Travel method for recovering from a specific DML operation — it doesn't require knowing the exact timestamp and avoids including changes made by other statements between the MERGE and a fixed offset.

questions.question: What is the maximum Time Travel retention period for a Transient table in Snowflake Enterprise edition?
options: 90 days — same as Permanent tables on Enterprise
7 days — Fail-Safe period
1 day — Transient tables are limited to a maximum of 1 day regardless of edition
Time Travel is not available for Transient tables
questions.explanation: Transient tables support Time Travel of only 0 or 1 day maximum, regardless of Snowflake edition. Enterprise edition's 90-day Time Travel applies ONLY to Permanent tables. This is a key distinction frequently tested on the exam: Transient = max 1 day TT + NO Fail-Safe.

questions.question: A company uses database replication to maintain a secondary Snowflake account for disaster recovery. The primary account goes down. What must happen for the secondary account to become writable?
options: The secondary account is always writable — replication is bidirectional
A Failover operation must be executed to promote the secondary account as the new primary
The DBA must manually re-create all objects in the secondary account
Snowflake automatically promotes the secondary when the primary is unreachable for more than 5 minutes
questions.explanation: Snowflake database replication creates read-only replicas in secondary accounts. To make the secondary writable (i.e., to failover), a Failover Group must be configured and a manual ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... FAILOVER command must be executed. This promotes the secondary to primary. Snowflake does not perform automatic failover.

questions.question: A developer accidentally runs DROP TABLE sales_2024. Time Travel DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. They run UNDROP TABLE sales_2024 two weeks and 1 day later. What happens?
options: The table is successfully restored — Fail-Safe extends the recovery window to 21 days total
UNDROP fails — the 14-day Time Travel window has expired; the table is in Fail-Safe but only Snowflake Support can recover it
UNDROP succeeds — Snowflake extends Time Travel automatically when a critical table is dropped
UNDROP fails — dropped tables cannot be recovered after 7 days on any edition
questions.explanation: UNDROP TABLE uses Time Travel. With DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14, the table can be recovered via UNDROP for 14 days from when it was dropped. After 14 days, the table enters Fail-Safe (7 additional days for permanent tables) but Fail-Safe is NOT user-accessible — only Snowflake Support can attempt recovery from Fail-Safe.

questions.question: What objects in Snowflake can be cloned using zero-copy cloning?
options: Only tables
Tables, schemas, and databases
Tables, schemas, databases, streams, tasks, stages, file formats, sequences, and pipes
All Snowflake objects including virtual warehouses and users
questions.explanation: Snowflake's zero-copy cloning supports: Tables, Schemas, Databases, Streams, Tasks, Stages, File Formats, Sequences, and Pipes. External tables, views, and warehouse objects cannot be cloned in the same way. Cloning a database or schema automatically clones all clonable objects within it.

questions.question: How does Fail-Safe differ from Time Travel in terms of user accessibility and purpose?
options: Fail-Safe is user-accessible for 7 days; Time Travel is Snowflake Support-only and for long-term recovery
Time Travel is a user-accessible self-service recovery feature; Fail-Safe is a non-configurable 7-day disaster recovery window accessible only by Snowflake Support
Both are user-accessible; Fail-Safe just has a longer retention period
They are the same feature with different names used in different Snowflake editions
questions.explanation: Time Travel is a self-service feature: users can query historical data with AT/BEFORE syntax, clone from historical state, and use UNDROP — accessible through normal SQL. Fail-Safe is an emergency disaster recovery mechanism: its data can only be accessed and restored by Snowflake Support, it cannot be queried by users, and it is non-configurable at exactly 7 days.

questions.question: A company clones their production database for a developer test environment. The dev team drops a large table in the clone. What is the storage impact on the production database?
options: Dropping the table in the clone immediately frees micro-partitions shared with production, reducing production storage
Dropping the table in the clone has no effect on production — the clone's micro-partitions are independent after the clone was created
Production storage increases because Snowflake creates a backup of the dropped table from the clone
Production's Time Travel storage increases because the clone drop triggers a Time Travel snapshot
questions.explanation: Zero-copy clone storage is independent per clone. At clone creation, both the clone and source share the same micro-partitions (zero additional storage). When the dev team drops a table in the clone, only the clone's reference to those shared micro-partitions is removed. The production database is completely unaffected — it retains its own references to the original micro-partitions.

questions.question: A Snowflake account needs to replicate not just data (databases) but also account-level objects like roles, users, warehouses, and security policies to a secondary account. Which Snowflake feature handles account-level object replication?
options: Database Replication Groups — they replicate everything in a database including account objects
Failover Groups — they can replicate both databases and account-level objects (integrations, roles, warehouses, policies) for business continuity
Account Cloning — creates a complete copy of the account in another region
ACCOUNT_USAGE replication — syncs metadata to the secondary account
questions.explanation: Failover Groups extend the replication scope beyond databases to include account-level objects: security integrations, network policies, resource monitors, roles, users, warehouses, and other account configuration. This enables a secondary account to become fully operational as a primary with all the same configuration when failover is triggered.

questions.question: In Snowflake Secure Data Sharing, what happens to data when a consumer account queries a shared table?
options: Snowflake copies the requested rows to the consumer's storage before the query executes
The consumer's virtual warehouse reads the shared data directly from the provider's cloud object storage — no data movement occurs
The provider's virtual warehouse processes the query and returns only the result to the consumer
Snowflake creates a temporary cache copy in the consumer's region to reduce latency
questions.explanation: Snowflake Secure Data Sharing is a zero-copy architecture. When a consumer queries shared data, their virtual warehouse reads micro-partitions directly from the provider's cloud object storage. No data is moved, copied, or cached in the consumer's account. The provider's storage billing covers the shared data; the consumer only pays for their compute.

questions.question: A data provider wants to share a table that contains sensitive information. They want consumers to only see rows relevant to their account and have specific columns masked. What is the recommended approach?
options: Share the raw table and instruct consumers to apply their own filters
Create a Secure View with Row Access Policy and Dynamic Data Masking applied, then share the Secure View
Share only a subset of columns using column-level sharing configuration
Use a Data Clean Room to restrict access
questions.explanation: For secure, governed sharing, the recommended pattern is: (1) Apply Row Access Policy and Dynamic Data Masking to control what each consumer can see, (2) create a Secure View over the table (which hides the underlying logic from consumers), (3) grant the Secure View to the Share. Consumers see only their permitted rows and masked column values.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Provider account and a Consumer account in Snowflake data sharing?
options: Provider accounts can only read data; Consumer accounts can write data to shares
Provider accounts create Share objects and define what data to share; Consumer accounts create databases from Share objects and query shared data using their own compute
Provider and Consumer are just labels — both accounts have equal capability in the sharing relationship
Providers must be on Enterprise edition; Consumers can be on any edition
questions.explanation: In Snowflake data sharing: the Provider creates the SHARE object, adds tables/views/schemas to it, and adds Consumer account identifiers. The Consumer uses CREATE DATABASE ... FROM SHARE to mount the shared data as a read-only database in their account and queries it using their own virtual warehouse — paying only for their compute.

questions.question: A Reader Account consumer completes their analysis project. Who is responsible for managing and deleting the Reader Account?
options: The consumer — they control their own Reader Account since it was created for them
The Provider — they created and manage the Reader Account using the ACCOUNTADMIN role in their own account
Snowflake Support — Reader Accounts can only be deleted by Snowflake
The Snowflake Marketplace — Reader Accounts are managed through the Marketplace admin portal
questions.explanation: Reader Accounts are created, administered, and deleted by the Provider using the MANAGE ACCOUNTS privilege (typically ACCOUNTADMIN). The Provider creates user credentials for consumers and controls all aspects of the Reader Account's lifecycle. The consumer has no direct control over the account's administration.

questions.question: Which type of Snowflake object CANNOT be directly included in a Share?
options: Tables
Secure Views
Schemas
Non-secure (standard) Views
questions.explanation: Standard (non-secure) Views cannot be added to a Share because they expose the underlying SQL definition (DDL) to consumers, which would reveal the provider's data model, table names, and business logic. Only Secure Views, tables, and secure UDFs can be included in a Share. Schemas can be granted but individual objects within them must be appropriate types.

questions.question: A Snowflake Data Clean Room allows two parties to collaborate on analysis. What makes Clean Rooms privacy-preserving?
options: Both parties must sign an NDA before accessing the Clean Room
The Clean Room environment restricts what queries can run — only pre-defined aggregation queries that prevent raw data exposure are allowed, so neither party can see the other's individual records
Clean Rooms encrypt all data with the other party's key, requiring mutual key exchange for decryption
Clean Room queries are reviewed by a neutral third-party auditor before results are released
questions.explanation: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms use a governed environment where only pre-approved analyses (typically aggregations and match results) can be executed. The technical controls prevent either party from writing arbitrary queries that could expose individual-level records. Neither party sees the other's raw data — only the analysis results of the joint dataset.

questions.question: When sharing data across regions in Snowflake (e.g., provider on AWS us-east-1, consumer on AWS eu-west-1), what must the provider configure first?
options: Enable cross-region sharing in the account settings — it is disabled by default
Create a replication of the database in the consumer's region first, then share from the replicated copy
Install Snowflake's cross-region gateway in both regions
Cross-region sharing is not supported — both accounts must be in the same region
questions.explanation: Cross-region Secure Data Sharing requires the provider to first replicate their database to the consumer's region using database replication. The consumer then mounts the replicated copy as a shared database. Sharing directly across regions without replication is not supported — the data must be in the same region as the consumer.

questions.question: Can a Consumer account reshare data they received from a Provider via Secure Data Sharing?
options: No — consumers can only read shared data, never reshare it
Yes — consumers can always reshare any data they receive
Yes — but only if the Provider explicitly grants resharing rights to the Consumer
Only if the Consumer is on Business Critical edition or higher
questions.explanation: Resharing is possible only when the Provider explicitly grants resharing rights. The Provider controls this permission and can restrict it. A Consumer who has been granted resharing rights can create their own Share and include objects from the Provider's shared data. Without explicit permission, resharing is not allowed.

questions.question: What is a 'listing' on the Snowflake Marketplace?
options: A SQL query that lists all available data products
A published data product or application that providers make available to consumers, described with metadata, pricing, and access configuration
A Snowflake account registered as a data provider on the platform
A catalog entry in the ACCOUNT_USAGE.MARKETPLACE_EVENTS view
questions.explanation: A Marketplace listing is a provider's published data product or application. It includes a description, data sample, category, pricing model, and the underlying Share or Native App. Consumers browse listings, request access, and once approved, mount the data as a shared database in their own Snowflake account.

questions.question: A data provider publishes weather data and wants all Snowflake customers to be able to discover and request access. Which listing type should they create?
options: Private listing — accessible to specific named accounts
Public listing — discoverable and accessible to any Snowflake account
Internal listing — visible only within the provider's organization
Premium listing — requires subscription approval
questions.explanation: A Public listing on the Snowflake Marketplace is visible to and requestable by any Snowflake account globally. It appears in the Marketplace catalog where any Snowflake user can discover, browse, and request access. Private listings are only visible to specifically designated consumer accounts.

questions.question: A software company builds an analytics application using Snowflake and wants to sell it to enterprise customers who already use Snowflake. The app analyzes the customer's own data inside their Snowflake account. Which distribution model is most appropriate?
options: Secure Data Sharing — share the analytics logic as a view
Snowflake Native App — package the application using the Native App Framework and distribute via the Marketplace
External Function — deploy logic externally and call it from customer SQL
Reader Account — create a managed environment for each customer
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Native App Framework allows ISVs to build and distribute applications that run natively inside consumer Snowflake accounts. The app's logic, Snowpark code, and UI (Streamlit) execute on the consumer's infrastructure, analyzing their own data without it leaving their account. Distributed via Marketplace listings.

questions.question: How does a Snowflake consumer access data from a free Marketplace listing after being approved?
options: The data is automatically downloaded to the consumer's internal stages
The consumer runs CREATE DATABASE <name> FROM SHARE <provider_account>.<share_name> to create a read-only database in their account
The consumer receives an email with data export files
The data appears automatically in the consumer's account without any action needed
questions.explanation: After a Marketplace listing is approved/installed, the consumer creates a database from the underlying Share using: CREATE DATABASE <db_name> FROM SHARE <provider_account_identifier>.<share_name>. This creates a read-only database in the consumer's account that references the provider's live data — the zero-copy sharing model.

questions.question: A developer creates a Standard View and then creates a Secure View containing identical SQL. A data consumer can access both. What difference will they observe?
options: No difference — secure and standard views return identical results for all users
Standard view DDL is visible via SHOW VIEWS or DESCRIBE VIEW; the Secure View's DDL is hidden from the consumer
The Secure View returns results faster because it uses the query result cache preferentially
The Secure View allows the consumer to modify the underlying data; the Standard View does not
questions.explanation: The key practical difference for a consumer: with a Standard View, the consumer can run SHOW VIEWS or GET_DDL() to see the underlying SQL (table names, business logic). With a Secure View, SHOW VIEWS shows the view exists but GET_DDL() and SHOW CREATE VIEW are blocked for non-owners — the underlying logic is protected.

questions.question: An administrator needs to revoke all privileges a role has on a specific schema. What is the most efficient approach?
options: Drop the role and recreate it without the schema privileges
Run REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA my_schema FROM ROLE my_role
Run individual REVOKE statements for each privilege type (SELECT, INSERT, etc.) on every object in the schema
Grant OWNERSHIP on the schema to a different role, which automatically revokes the original role's privileges
questions.explanation: REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA my_schema FROM ROLE my_role revokes all schema-level privileges in a single command. This is more efficient than individual REVOKE statements per privilege. Note: this revokes schema-level grants but may not cascade to object-level grants within the schema — those may require separate REVOKE ALL ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA.

questions.question: A Snowflake Task is created but no queries are being processed. An administrator checks and finds the task status is 'SUSPENDED'. What command resumes the task?
options: START TASK my_task;
RESUME TASK my_task;
ALTER TASK my_task RESUME;
