ALTER TASK my_task SET STATUS = ACTIVE;
questions.explanation: Snowflake Tasks are created in a SUSPENDED state by default and must be explicitly started using ALTER TASK <task_name> RESUME. This activates the task and allows it to run on its defined schedule. START TASK and RESUME TASK are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.question: A table has 10 billion rows and a clustering key on ORDER_DATE. After 2 years of daily inserts and updates, queries that were fast are now slow again. What should be investigated first?
options: The clustering key definition — it may need to be changed to a different column
Clustering depth — heavy DML may have increased the average depth, degrading clustering quality and requiring reclustering
The warehouse size — it may need to be doubled to compensate for the larger table
The Time Travel retention — reducing it may improve query performance
questions.explanation: Heavy DML over time degrades clustering because new micro-partitions are written without maintaining the original sort order. Run SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION() to check the average_depth. If it has increased significantly from ~1.0, Automatic Clustering or a manual RECLUSTER operation will reorganize partitions and restore performance.

questions.question: What happens to a Snowflake zero-copy clone when the SOURCE TABLE is dropped?
options: The clone is also automatically dropped because it depended on the source
The clone remains intact and independent — it has its own references to the shared micro-partitions
The clone becomes read-only — it can be queried but no new writes are allowed
All shared micro-partitions are moved to the clone's storage billing
questions.explanation: Zero-copy clones are fully independent objects. When a clone is created, it gets its own metadata references to the shared micro-partitions. Dropping the source table removes the source's metadata but the clone retains its own independent references to those micro-partitions. The clone continues to work normally.

questions.question: A Snowflake governance team applies object tags to classify data across 500 tables. Which view in ACCOUNT_USAGE allows querying all tag-to-object associations across the account?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.OBJECT_TAGS
ACCOUNT_USAGE.TAG_REFERENCES
ACCOUNT_USAGE.COLUMN_TAGS
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TAG_ASSOCIATIONS
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.TAG_REFERENCES is the view that tracks all tag assignments across the account. It shows which tag (TAG_NAME, TAG_VALUE) is applied to which object (OBJECT_DATABASE, OBJECT_SCHEMA, OBJECT_NAME, COLUMN_NAME) and at what domain level (TABLE, COLUMN, SCHEMA, DATABASE). It is the central catalog for tag-based governance.

questions.question: A data team wants to use Snowflake Cortex to extract structured information (product names, prices) from unstructured product descriptions stored in a VARCHAR column. Which is the most appropriate Cortex function?
options: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT() — it extracts sentiment and entities simultaneously
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE() with a custom extraction prompt
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.CLASSIFY_TEXT() with product-related categories
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SUMMARIZE() with entity extraction mode
questions.explanation: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE() is the general-purpose LLM function that accepts a custom prompt. For structured extraction from unstructured text, you would provide a prompt like 'Extract product name and price from: {description}. Return JSON.' SENTIMENT(), CLASSIFY_TEXT(), and SUMMARIZE() are specialized functions for specific tasks and don't support custom extraction prompts.

questions.question: A Snowflake query uses LISTAGG(product_name, ', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY product_name). What does this return?
options: A count of products ordered alphabetically
A comma-separated string of product names in alphabetical order, aggregated per group
An array of product names in alphabetical order
The product name that appears most frequently in the group
questions.explanation: LISTAGG(col, delimiter) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY col) is an aggregate function that concatenates values from multiple rows into a single delimited string. WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY product_name) ensures the values are concatenated in alphabetical order. The result is a VARCHAR string like 'Apple, Banana, Cherry'. It's Snowflake's equivalent of GROUP_CONCAT (MySQL) or STRING_AGG (PostgreSQL/SQL Server).

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view tracks credit consumption specifically from Automatic Clustering (background micro-partition reorganisation)?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.CLUSTERING_COST
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY records serverless credit consumption for each table that has Automatic Clustering enabled, broken down by table and time window. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only tracks virtual warehouse credits. METERING_HISTORY is a roll-up of all serverless features combined.

questions.question: A FinOps analyst needs to see the serverless credits consumed by Materialized View background refresh operations. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view contains this information?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.MATERIALIZED_VIEW_REFRESH_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'MATERIALIZED_VIEW'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY filtered on MV warehouse
Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: Both ACCOUNT_USAGE.MATERIALIZED_VIEW_REFRESH_HISTORY (per-MV granularity) and ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY (filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'MATERIALIZED_VIEW') provide Materialized View refresh credit data. MATERIALIZED_VIEW_REFRESH_HISTORY gives the most detailed per-view breakdown.

questions.question: A team runs hundreds of serverless Snowflake Tasks per hour. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view shows the credits consumed per serverless task execution?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY records each execution of a serverless task (Tasks configured with USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE instead of a named warehouse), including the credits consumed per run. TASK_HISTORY shows task execution status and timing but not serverless credit usage.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view tracks credits consumed by the Query Acceleration Service (QAS) on an hourly basis?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — QAS credits appear here alongside warehouse credits
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_ACCELERATION_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'QAS'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered on QAS-eligible queries
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_ACCELERATION_HISTORY provides hourly credit consumption for the Query Acceleration Service per warehouse. QAS is billed separately from virtual warehouse credits. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY covers only the warehouse's own compute, not QAS serverless credits.

questions.question: A company replicates databases to a secondary Snowflake region for disaster recovery. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view tracks the credits consumed by database replication?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.FAILOVER_CREDITS
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY records credits consumed by database replication operations (both primary and secondary) and data transfer volumes. METERING_HISTORY is an aggregate roll-up; REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-replication-group granularity.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view helps identify unexpected data egress costs by tracking bytes transferred between Snowflake and external locations?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_TRANSFER_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.EGRESS_USAGE
ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records data transfer events between Snowflake and external locations, including bytes transferred and destination region. This helps identify cross-region data movement or external function calls that incur egress charges. STORAGE_USAGE only covers storage bytes, not transfer.

questions.question: A Resource Monitor is created with NOTIFY_USERS = ('admin@company.com', 'finops@company.com'). What does this parameter control?
options: The list of Snowflake roles that can modify the Resource Monitor
The email addresses that receive notifications when threshold actions are triggered
The list of warehouse administrators who can override the monitor's suspend action
The users whose queries are tracked by this specific Resource Monitor
questions.explanation: The NOTIFY_USERS parameter on a Resource Monitor specifies which users receive email notifications when the monitor's threshold actions fire. The email addresses must correspond to Snowflake user accounts with a registered email address. This is separate from the list of users the monitor applies to.

questions.question: A Snowflake account has two Resource Monitors: one at the account level (quota=10,000) and one assigned specifically to warehouse DW_ETL (quota=2,000). What happens when DW_ETL reaches 2,000 credits?
options: Only the account-level monitor fires — warehouse monitors are ignored when an account monitor exists
Both monitors trigger simultaneously — the warehouse monitor's action fires on DW_ETL, and both quotas count the same credits
The warehouse-level monitor triggers its action for DW_ETL only — the account monitor continues counting toward its own 10,000 limit
The lower quota always wins — the account monitor's 10,000 is ignored once the warehouse monitor fires
questions.explanation: Warehouse-level Resource Monitors operate independently of account-level monitors. When DW_ETL reaches its 2,000 credit quota, the warehouse monitor's action (NOTIFY, SUSPEND, etc.) fires for that warehouse only. The account-level monitor independently tracks all account credits toward its own 10,000 quota.

questions.question: How can a Snowflake Organization admin view credit consumption aggregated across ALL accounts in the organisation?
options: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY — it automatically includes all org accounts
Query SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY from the organization admin account
Use the ACCOUNTADMIN role and run SHOW ORGANIZATION CREDITS
Each account's ACCOUNT_USAGE must be queried separately — no cross-account aggregation exists
questions.explanation: The SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema (accessible to ORGADMIN) provides cross-account views including USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY which shows daily spend in currency across all accounts in the organisation. Individual ACCOUNT_USAGE views are scoped to a single account and cannot be queried cross-account.

questions.question: A multi-cluster warehouse scales from 1 to 3 clusters for 2 hours to handle peak load, then returns to 1 cluster. It is a Large warehouse (8 credits/hour). How many total credits were consumed during the 2-hour peak period?
options: 16 credits (8 × 2 hours, only base cluster billed)
32 credits (8 × 2 hours × 2 extra clusters only)
48 credits (8 credits/hour × 3 clusters × 2 hours)
24 credits (8 × 2 hours + 4 credits surcharge per extra cluster)
questions.explanation: Each active cluster in a multi-cluster warehouse is billed at the full warehouse rate. With 3 clusters running: 8 credits/hour × 3 clusters × 2 hours = 48 credits. Each cluster runs independently and is billed as a full separate warehouse. There is no 'surcharge' model — each cluster costs exactly one warehouse-size's worth of credits.

questions.question: What does the SERVICE_TYPE column in ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY identify?
options: The cloud provider service (AWS, Azure, or GCP) that processed the credits
The Snowflake feature category (WAREHOUSE, SNOWPIPE, AUTO_CLUSTERING, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, etc.) that consumed the credits
The user role that triggered the compute operation
Whether the credits were for storage or compute
questions.explanation: METERING_HISTORY.SERVICE_TYPE identifies which Snowflake feature consumed the credits. Common values include: WAREHOUSE (virtual warehouse compute), AUTO_CLUSTERING (Automatic Clustering), MATERIALIZED_VIEW (MV refresh), SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION (SOS maintenance), PIPE (Snowpipe), QUERY_ACCELERATION (QAS). This enables cost attribution by feature type.

questions.question: A FinOps team wants to reduce idle warehouse credit waste. Which TWO warehouse parameters should they review and potentially lower for infrequently used warehouses?
options: AUTO_SUSPEND (reduce the idle timeout before suspension) and MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL (limit concurrent queries to save credits)
AUTO_SUSPEND (reduce idle timeout to suspend sooner) and STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS (kill runaway queries faster to avoid wasted compute time)
MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT (reduce always-on clusters) and WAREHOUSE_SIZE (downsize to XS when possible for light workloads)
AUTO_RESUME (disable it to prevent unplanned starts) and QUERY_TAG (tag idle queries for tracking)
questions.explanation: Lowering AUTO_SUSPEND reduces idle time before the warehouse suspends (saving credits on idle periods). Lowering STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS kills runaway long-running queries, preventing them from holding the warehouse active and consuming credits unproductively. Both directly reduce wasted compute spend.

questions.question: Which view in the SNOWFLAKE shared database provides near-real-time visibility of currently running queries across the account, useful for live cost monitoring dashboards?
options: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — updated in real-time
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACTIVE_QUERIES
Information Schema's QUERY_HISTORY table function (e.g., TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY()))
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY
questions.explanation: The INFORMATION_SCHEMA table functions (QUERY_HISTORY(), QUERY_HISTORY_BY_USER(), QUERY_HISTORY_BY_SESSION()) provide near-real-time query data with minimal latency. ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY has 45-minute latency. ACTIVE_QUERIES is not an official Snowflake view. WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY shows load over time intervals.

questions.question: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY provides data at what granularity?
options: Per individual query execution
Per-minute averages of warehouse load (queued queries, active queries, blocked queries)
Per-hour total credits per warehouse
Per-day average warehouse utilisation percentage
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY provides per-minute averages of three load metrics per warehouse: AVG_RUNNING (average concurrent running queries), AVG_QUEUED_LOAD (average queued queries), and AVG_BLOCKED (average blocked queries). This helps identify peak load periods and capacity planning needs.

questions.question: A data platform team wants to automatically alert when any single query on the ANALYTICS warehouse exceeds 10 minutes of execution time. What is the correct Snowflake-native approach?
options: Set STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 600 on the warehouse — this automatically sends an email notification and kills the query

Create a Snowflake Alert that queries INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY for queries with EXECUTION_STATUS = 'RUNNING' and TOTAL_ELAPSED_TIME > 600000 ms, and sends a notification via SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL()
Configure a Resource Monitor with SUSPEND at 1 credit to block long queries
Enable Query Acceleration Service — it automatically terminates queries exceeding 10 minutes
questions.explanation: A Snowflake Alert with a condition querying INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY for long-running queries and an action calling SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL() is the correct native alerting approach. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT only kills queries silently — it does not send email notifications. Resource Monitors track credits, not query duration.

questions.question: A 2XL warehouse (32 credits/hour) runs a single query for exactly 90 seconds then auto-suspends. How many credits are billed?
options: 0.8 credits (32 × 90/3600 prorated)
32 credits (full hour minimum)
1.07 credits (32 × 2 minutes / 60)
~1.07 credits — 90 seconds rounds up to 2 billing minutes: 32 credits/hr × 2/60
questions.explanation: Snowflake bills in 1-minute increments with a 1-minute minimum. 90 seconds exceeds the first minute so it rounds up to 2 billing minutes. Credits = 32 credits/hour × (2 minutes / 60 minutes) = 32 × 0.0333 ≈ 1.07 credits. There is no per-second proration after the 1-minute minimum.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Resource Monitor set at the ACCOUNT level versus one assigned to a specific WAREHOUSE?
options: Account-level monitors are free; warehouse-level monitors consume 1 extra credit per day
Account-level monitors track aggregate credits across ALL warehouses in the account; warehouse-level monitors track only the assigned warehouse's credits
Warehouse-level monitors can only use NOTIFY actions; account-level monitors support all three actions
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can create account-level monitors; SYSADMIN creates warehouse-level monitors
questions.explanation: A Resource Monitor assigned to a warehouse only tracks and enforces limits for that specific warehouse. An account-level Resource Monitor (created without the WAREHOUSES clause) tracks all warehouse credit usage across the entire account. This is the fundamental operational difference between the two scopes.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view shows the history of Snowflake Alerts that have fired, including their trigger time and action result?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.ALERT_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'ALERT'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY (Alerts are implemented as special Tasks)
ACCOUNT_USAGE.NOTIFICATION_HISTORY
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.ALERT_HISTORY records the execution history of Snowflake Alerts, including the alert name, scheduled time, trigger condition evaluation, action taken, and execution state. This allows auditing when alerts fired and whether their actions completed successfully.

questions.question: Which SQL correctly calculates the total credits consumed by ALL serverless features (Snowpipe, Automatic Clustering, MV refresh, QAS, etc.) over the last 30 days?
options: SELECT SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME > DATEADD('day', -30, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
SELECT SERVICE_TYPE, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME > DATEADD('day', -30, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) AND SERVICE_TYPE != 'WAREHOUSE' GROUP BY 1
SELECT SUM(SERVERLESS_CREDITS) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_USAGE WHERE MONTH = CURRENT_MONTH()
SELECT SUM(CREDITS) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.BILLING_HISTORY WHERE TYPE = 'SERVERLESS'
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY contains credits for all Snowflake features by SERVICE_TYPE. Filtering out SERVICE_TYPE = 'WAREHOUSE' leaves only serverless feature credits (SNOWPIPE, AUTO_CLUSTERING, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION, etc.). WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only has warehouse credits. SERVERLESS_USAGE and BILLING_HISTORY are not official view names.

questions.question: A FinOps engineer finds that Automatic Clustering costs are unexpectedly high on a 500 GB fact table. Which action DIRECTLY reduces Automatic Clustering credit consumption on that table?
options: Increase the warehouse size — larger warehouses process reclustering faster, using fewer total credits
Suspend or disable Automatic Clustering on the table using ALTER TABLE t SUSPEND RECLUSTER
Reduce the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table — shorter Time Travel reduces reclustering scope
Change the clustering key to a lower-cardinality column — this has no effect on Automatic Clustering cost
questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE <table> SUSPEND RECLUSTER stops Automatic Clustering background operations on that table, immediately halting the associated serverless credit consumption. Changing or adding better clustering keys can also reduce long-term reclustering cost, but the most direct immediate action is suspending reclustering.

questions.question: A Snowflake account is billed $0 for Cloud Services in a month where the account ran 500 warehouse-hours of compute. Why might this be?
options: Cloud Services is always free — Snowflake does not charge for it
Cloud Services costs were below the 10% daily compute credit threshold, so the overage was waived under the free tier
The ACCOUNTADMIN disabled Cloud Services billing for the account
Cloud Services costs only apply to Enterprise and above editions
questions.explanation: Snowflake provides a Cloud Services free tier: daily Cloud Services usage up to 10% of the day's warehouse compute credits is included at no extra cost. If Cloud Services consumption stays under this threshold (which it typically does for normal workloads), there is no additional Cloud Services charge — it is absorbed into the compute cost.

questions.question: A warehouse is shared across 5 different teams, each using a different role. A FinOps analyst wants to see credit attribution per team. Which approach provides per-role credit breakdown from ACCOUNT_USAGE?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — it breaks down credits by ROLE_NAME per warehouse
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY grouped by ROLE_NAME — sum CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES per role
ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLE_METERING — the dedicated view for per-role credit attribution
Resource Monitors filter credits by role automatically when ROLES parameter is specified
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY includes the ROLE_NAME column for each query. Grouping by ROLE_NAME and summing CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES provides per-role Cloud Services attribution. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY aggregates at warehouse level with no role breakdown. ROLE_METERING is not a real Snowflake view.

questions.question: A Resource Monitor with START_TIMESTAMP set to a future date is created today. What happens to warehouse credits consumed BEFORE the start timestamp?
options: Credits before the start timestamp are counted retroactively toward the quota
The Resource Monitor does not track or count credits consumed before the START_TIMESTAMP — monitoring only begins from that date
The warehouse is suspended until the START_TIMESTAMP is reached
A START_TIMESTAMP cannot be set on a Resource Monitor — this parameter does not exist
questions.explanation: The START_TIMESTAMP parameter on a Resource Monitor defines when credit counting begins. Credits consumed before this timestamp are not counted toward the monitor's quota. This is useful for aligning monitors with billing periods or fiscal month start dates.

questions.question: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view is BEST for finding the 10 most storage-intensive tables in the account, ranked by total bytes including Time Travel and Fail-Safe overhead?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE — provides account-level totals only
ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS — provides ACTIVE_BYTES + TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES + FAILSAFE_BYTES per table
ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — cross-references storage per warehouse
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES — provides TABLE_BYTES per table
questions.explanation: TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS provides per-table byte breakdowns: ACTIVE_BYTES (current data), TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES (historical versions), FAILSAFE_BYTES, and RETAINED_FOR_CLONE_BYTES. Summing these gives total per-table storage cost. STORAGE_USAGE only gives account-level aggregates. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES has TABLE_BYTES but does not include Time Travel or Fail-Safe overhead.

questions.question: What does ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY record that WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY does not?
options: WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records warehouse lifecycle events: start, suspend, resize, and cluster add/remove; METERING_HISTORY records only credit consumption
WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records per-query timing; METERING_HISTORY records hourly totals
Both views record identical data — EVENTS_HISTORY is deprecated
WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records failed query events; METERING_HISTORY records successful ones
questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records warehouse lifecycle events such as STARTED, SUSPENDED, RESIZED, CREATED, and DROPPED, along with cluster scale-up and scale-down events for multi-cluster warehouses. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY records hourly credit consumption. Together they provide a complete operational and cost picture.

questions.question: A FinOps analyst notices that a table's FAILSAFE_BYTES in TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS is unexpectedly large. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: The table was recently loaded with a very large initial dataset
The table underwent heavy DML (many UPDATEs or DELETEs), creating many historical micro-partitions that passed through Time Travel into Fail-Safe
Fail-Safe bytes grow proportionally with the number of queries that read the table
The table has a large number of columns, which inflates Fail-Safe overhead
questions.explanation: Fail-Safe retains micro-partitions that have passed through the Time Travel window. Tables with frequent UPDATE and DELETE operations generate new micro-partitions while the old ones are retained through Time Travel and then Fail-Safe. Heavy DML over time creates a large historical footprint in Fail-Safe.

questions.question: How does Snowflake bill for Search Optimization Service (SOS) maintenance compute?
options: SOS maintenance runs on the user's virtual warehouse and is included in warehouse credits
SOS is completely free — there is no additional charge for maintaining search access paths
SOS maintenance uses serverless compute billed separately, visible in ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY with SERVICE_TYPE = 'SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION'
SOS charges a flat monthly fee based on the number of tables with search optimization enabled
questions.explanation: Search Optimization Service uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute to build and maintain search access paths. This is billed separately from virtual warehouse credits and appears in ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY (and specifically ACCOUNT_USAGE.SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION_HISTORY) with the SEARCH_OPTIMIZATION service type.

questions.question: An administrator accidentally drops a Resource Monitor that was protecting a high-cost warehouse. What immediately happens to that warehouse?
options: The warehouse is immediately suspended as a safety measure
The warehouse continues running with no credit limit enforcement — it is unprotected until a new Resource Monitor is assigned
The warehouse inherits the account-level Resource Monitor automatically
The warehouse is set to AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 as an emergency fallback
questions.explanation: When a Resource Monitor is dropped, any warehouses that were assigned to it immediately lose their credit limit protection. The warehouses continue operating without any credit cap until a new Resource Monitor is created and assigned. There is no automatic fallback or emergency protection triggered.

questions.question: A data platform team wants to create a monthly FinOps report showing: (1) total virtual warehouse credits, (2) total serverless feature credits, and (3) total storage TB-months. Which three ACCOUNT_USAGE views are needed?
options: QUERY_HISTORY, PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY, and TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY, METERING_HISTORY (serverless), and STORAGE_USAGE
WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY, AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY, and DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
BILLING_HISTORY, SERVERLESS_USAGE, and STORAGE_BILLING
questions.explanation: The correct three views are: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (virtual warehouse compute credits), METERING_HISTORY filtered for non-WAREHOUSE service types (all serverless feature credits: Snowpipe, Auto Clustering, MV refresh, SOS, QAS), and STORAGE_USAGE (daily average storage bytes which translates to TB-month billing). BILLING_HISTORY and SERVERLESS_USAGE are not official Snowflake view names.

questions.question: When a Resource Monitor triggers a SUSPEND action, which queries are affected?
options: All currently running queries are immediately killed and no new queries can start
No currently running queries are affected — they complete normally; only NEW queries submitted after the suspend threshold is reached are blocked
Only queued queries are cancelled; currently executing queries continue to completion
The suspend action takes effect at the start of the next billing minute
questions.explanation: SUSPEND (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE) is a graceful action. Currently running queries are allowed to complete. Only NEW queries submitted after the credit threshold is crossed are blocked. This is a critical distinction from SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE, which kills all running and queued queries immediately.

questions.question: A Snowflake administrator wants to understand the full cost of a specific business-critical pipeline that uses: a warehouse, Snowpipe loading, and Automatic Clustering. Which combination of views provides complete cost visibility for this pipeline?
options: QUERY_HISTORY (warehouse queries), PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY (Snowpipe credits), and AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY (clustering credits) — all filtered by relevant object names and time range
WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY alone — it aggregates all pipeline costs
METERING_HISTORY alone — it contains all cost types in one place
BILLING_HISTORY with pipeline_id filter parameter
questions.explanation: Three separate ACCOUNT_USAGE views are needed for complete pipeline cost visibility: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY or QUERY_HISTORY (warehouse compute for SQL transformations), PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY (Snowpipe serverless loading credits per pipe), and AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY (background clustering credits per table). Each feature bills independently and appears in its own view.

questions.question: Which Snowflake layer handles authentication, query optimization, and transaction management?
options: Storage layer
Virtual warehouse layer
Cloud services layer
Replication layer
questions.explanation: The cloud services layer is the brain of Snowflake. It handles authentication, metadata management, query parsing and optimization, and transaction management.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of separating storage from compute in Snowflake?
options: Lower network latency between warehouses
Multiple warehouses can read the same data concurrently without contention
Automatic cross-region replication
Reduced cloud provider fees
questions.explanation: Because storage is decoupled from compute, any number of virtual warehouses can simultaneously read the same data without locking or resource contention.

questions.question: What format does Snowflake use to store table data internally?
options: Row-oriented CSV files
Columnar micro-partitions in compressed, encrypted files
ORC files with Parquet metadata
Avro files organized by partition key
questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically divides table data into columnar micro-partitions — contiguous storage units of 50–500 MB uncompressed, stored compressed and encrypted.

questions.question: What happens to a virtual warehouse's local disk cache when it is suspended?
options: It is persisted to S3 for fast resume
It is cleared — the cache must be rebuilt from scratch on resume
It is saved in the cloud services layer
It remains intact for 24 hours
questions.explanation: Suspending a warehouse de-provisions all compute resources including local SSD cache. The cache starts cold on the next resume.

questions.question: Under what condition does Snowflake charge credits for cloud services usage?
options: Always — every query incurs a cloud services fee
When cloud services consumption exceeds 10% of daily compute credit usage
When more than 100 queries run per hour
Only for queries accessing external stages
questions.explanation: Cloud services are effectively free. Credits are only billed when daily cloud services usage exceeds 10% of daily warehouse credits — a threshold rarely reached in normal workloads.

questions.question: What does Snowflake's query optimizer use to skip micro-partitions?
options: Row count per partition
Min and max column values stored as metadata statistics per micro-partition
A full index of all column values
The clustering key definition only
questions.explanation: The optimizer reads min/max statistics per micro-partition. If a filter predicate cannot match any row in a partition's range, that partition is skipped entirely (data skipping / pruning).

questions.question: What is the billing model for Snowflake storage costs?
options: Per number of tables and views
Average daily data stored in TB, billed monthly
Per micro-partition created
Per DML operation performed
questions.explanation: Snowflake calculates the average daily storage across the month and bills per TB. This includes active data, Time Travel versions, and Fail-Safe data.

questions.question: What is Tri-Secret Secure in Snowflake?
options: Three-factor authentication for admin accounts
Encryption using both a Snowflake-managed key and a customer-managed key — neither party alone can decrypt data
A three-region replication topology
A feature requiring three approvers for data access requests
questions.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure combines Snowflake's master key with a customer-managed CMK (in AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, etc.). Both keys are required to decrypt data, so Snowflake cannot access customer data without the CMK.

questions.question: How does Snowflake handle an UPDATE at the storage level?
options: In-place update of existing micro-partition files
Old micro-partitions are dereferenced and new partitions with updated rows are written — no in-place mutation occurs
Changes are appended to a separate delta file
Rows are locked and column values overwritten
questions.explanation: Snowflake's storage is immutable. An UPDATE creates new micro-partitions with updated rows; old partitions are retained for Time Travel and eventually purged.

questions.question: What is the minimum billing period for a Snowflake virtual warehouse?
options: 1 second
10 seconds
60 seconds
5 minutes
questions.explanation: Warehouses are billed per second with a 60-second minimum. A run of 30 seconds is still billed as 60 seconds; after the first minute each additional second is billed precisely.

questions.question: Which Snowflake edition is required for HIPAA and PCI DSS compliance?
options: Standard
Enterprise
Business Critical
Virtual Private Snowflake
questions.explanation: Business Critical edition adds enhanced compliance certifications including HIPAA, PCI DSS, and SOC 1/2 Type II. Standard and Enterprise do not include these.

questions.question: What cloud storage service does Snowflake use for its storage layer on AWS?
options: AWS EBS
AWS S3
AWS EFS
AWS Glacier
questions.explanation: On AWS, Snowflake stores all table data in S3 buckets managed by Snowflake. Customers never access these buckets directly; Snowflake abstracts all S3 interactions.

questions.question: What is Snowsight?
options: A third-party SQL IDE with a Snowflake plugin
Snowflake's built-in web UI for querying, dashboards, and account management
A CLI tool for scripting Snowflake operations
A monitoring service provided by Snowflake Support
questions.explanation: Snowsight is Snowflake's modern web-based interface supporting SQL worksheets, query profiling, result visualization, dashboards, and account administration.

questions.question: What does the Snowflake Connector for Python provide?
options: Running Python UDFs inside Snowflake
A Python DB-API 2.0 compliant interface for connecting Python applications to Snowflake
Installing Snowflake on a Python server
Transpiling Python code to SQL
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Connector for Python implements the standard Python DB-API 2.0 interface, allowing Python applications to execute queries, load data, and manage Snowflake objects.

questions.question: How does a client authenticate to Snowflake using key-pair authentication?
options: By sending username and password in every request
By presenting a private key; the matching public key is registered on the Snowflake user account
By using an OAuth token from the cloud provider
By sending an API key in the Authorization header
questions.explanation: Key-pair authentication uses RSA keys. The public key is registered on the user via ALTER USER ... SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY. The client signs requests with the private key; Snowflake verifies using the registered public key.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Snowflake Connector for Kafka?
options: Exporting Snowflake query results to Kafka topics
Consuming records from Kafka topics and loading them into Snowflake tables in near-real time
Monitoring Snowflake query performance via Kafka Streams
Authenticating Snowflake users via Kafka's SASL mechanism
questions.explanation: The Snowflake Connector for Kafka is a Kafka Connect sink connector that reads records from Kafka topics and automatically loads them into Snowflake tables.

questions.question: What is the correct Snowflake object hierarchy from top to bottom?
options: Organization → Database → Account → Schema → Table
Organization → Account → Database → Schema → Table
Account → Database → Schema → Table → Organization
Account → Organization → Database → Schema → Table
questions.explanation: The full hierarchy is Organization → Account → Database → Schema → Table (and other schema-level objects like stages, views, and procedures).

questions.question: What is the key difference between a transient and a permanent table?
options: Transient tables do not support DML
Transient tables have no Fail-Safe period and a maximum 1-day Time Travel — reducing storage cost for staging data
Transient tables cannot be queried by other users
Transient tables are stored in memory only
questions.explanation: Permanent tables support up to 90 days Time Travel and 7 days Fail-Safe. Transient tables have 0 days Fail-Safe and maximum 1-day Time Travel — ideal for intermediate staging where lower cost matters.

questions.question: Which Snowflake object records row-level changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on a source table for CDC pipelines?
options: Task
Pipe
Stream
Event table
questions.explanation: A Stream is a CDC object that records the net changes made to a source table since the stream was last consumed, enabling incremental data pipelines.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake dynamic table?
options: A table whose schema changes automatically based on incoming data
A declaratively defined table that Snowflake automatically refreshes incrementally based on a SQL query and a configurable target lag
A table that switches between row and columnar storage
A table that auto-partitions by a date column
questions.explanation: Dynamic tables let you define a target lag and a SQL query; Snowflake handles incremental refresh automatically — a simpler alternative to stream-task pipelines for many transformation use cases.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake file format object?
options: A stored procedure that formats query output
A reusable named object defining the structure of data files (CSV, JSON, Parquet, etc.) used in COPY INTO operations
A table column type for storing binary files
A stage configuration that determines upload compression
questions.explanation: A file format object stores parsing and formatting options for a specific data type. Referencing a named file format avoids repeating delimiter, compression, and NULL-handling options in every COPY INTO.

questions.question: What does CREATE TABLE ... CLONE achieve?
options: Creates an independent physical copy of all data immediately
Creates a logical copy sharing source micro-partitions at no additional storage cost — new storage is charged only as data diverges (copy-on-write)
Creates a read-only reference to the source table
Creates a backup inaccessible for 7 days
questions.explanation: Zero-Copy Clone uses Snowflake's copy-on-write mechanism. The clone immediately shares source micro-partitions at no storage cost. Storage costs arise only when either copy is modified.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake SHARE object?
options: A role that can be assigned to external users
A named object that bundles databases, schemas, tables, or views for read-only access by consumer accounts — without copying data
A public table accessible to all accounts
A read replica of a database in another region
questions.explanation: A SHARE is the foundation of Snowflake Secure Data Sharing. Providers grant object privileges to the share; consumers access a read-only virtual database backed by the provider's live data.

questions.question: What is the INFORMATION_SCHEMA in Snowflake?
options: A special role with read access to all tables
A set of read-only views within each database exposing metadata about tables, columns, stages, and other objects
A separate paid monitoring service
A Snowflake-managed schema storing Time Travel history
questions.explanation: INFORMATION_SCHEMA is an ANSI-standard set of views in every Snowflake database exposing current-state metadata with near-zero latency.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake Alert object?
options: A Task that sends periodic emails
A Snowflake object that evaluates a SQL condition on a schedule and sends a notification if the condition is true
A resource monitor with email notifications
A Snowpipe error notification
questions.explanation: An Alert evaluates a SQL condition on a schedule. When the condition returns TRUE, it triggers a notification through a configured integration (email, Slack, SNS, etc.).

questions.question: What is the credit cost per hour of a Large warehouse relative to an X-Small?
options: 2x more
4x more
8x more
16x more
questions.explanation: Warehouse sizes double in credits per step: XS=1, S=2, M=4, L=8. A Large costs 8 credits/hour — 8x an X-Small.

questions.question: What does AUTO_SUSPEND on a virtual warehouse do?
options: Suspends the warehouse if it runs longer than the specified number of minutes
Suspends the warehouse after the specified number of seconds of inactivity, preventing idle credit consumption
Automatically scales down the warehouse size when idle
Cancels queries running longer than the timeout value
questions.explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND specifies inactivity seconds after which Snowflake automatically suspends the warehouse. This is the primary mechanism for controlling idle credit waste.

questions.question: Which Snowflake edition is required to use multi-cluster virtual warehouses?
options: Standard
Enterprise or higher
Business Critical
Virtual Private Snowflake
questions.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses (MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT > 1) require Enterprise edition or higher. Standard edition supports only single-cluster warehouses.

questions.question: What is concurrency scaling in Snowflake?
options: Manually adding warehouses when load increases
Automatically spinning up additional transient clusters when the primary warehouse queue grows — the first 1 credit per account per day is free
Increasing warehouse size when CPU utilization exceeds 80%
Routing queries to the nearest regional cluster for low latency
questions.explanation: Concurrency scaling automatically adds cluster capacity when queuing grows. Each account gets 1 free credit per day of concurrency scaling; additional usage is billed at standard rates.

questions.question: Which parameter limits how long a SQL statement can run before it is automatically cancelled?
options: QUERY_TIMEOUT
STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
MAX_RUNTIME_SECONDS
STATEMENT_QUEUED_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
questions.explanation: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS limits single statement execution time. It can be set at the warehouse, session, or account level. Exceeded statements are automatically cancelled.

questions.question: What is the purpose of resource monitors in Snowflake?
options: Monitor CPU and memory utilization on virtual warehouses
Track credit consumption against quotas and take configured actions (notify, suspend) when thresholds are breached, preventing cost overruns
Monitor data quality metrics on tables
Alert when Time Travel storage exceeds a threshold
questions.explanation: A resource monitor tracks credit usage against a configured quota and can trigger actions — notify, suspend, or suspend immediately — at configured percentage thresholds.

questions.question: When does a virtual warehouse begin consuming credits?
options: Only when actively executing queries
From the moment it resumes until it is suspended, regardless of whether queries are running
Only during the first minute of each run
After the 60-second minimum billing period has elapsed
questions.explanation: Once resumed, a warehouse consumes credits continuously until suspended — whether or not queries are running. AUTO_SUSPEND is critical to avoid idle credit drain.

questions.question: What is a Snowpark-optimized warehouse?
options: A warehouse with more nodes for higher concurrency
A warehouse type with nodes having 16x more memory per node than standard — designed for memory-intensive ML training and large Snowpark workloads
A warehouse that uses GPUs for accelerated computation
A warehouse that cannot run standard SQL queries
questions.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses (WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED) have dramatically higher memory-to-CPU ratios, designed for memory-intensive Snowpark Python workloads like ML model training.

questions.question: What is the Query Acceleration Service (QAS)?
options: A CDN-like cache layer for query results
A serverless feature that offloads eligible query portions (large scans, aggregations) to elastic compute, primarily benefiting long-running queries with large data scans
A query rewrite service that optimizes SQL before execution
A dedicated result cache for high-cardinality aggregations
questions.explanation: QAS automatically offloads portions of eligible queries to Snowflake-managed serverless compute. Enabled per warehouse with ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE. Most effective for large scan and aggregation queries.

questions.question: A company wants to prevent BI reports from competing with ETL jobs for compute resources. What is the recommended Snowflake approach?
options: Use resource monitors to throttle one workload
Create separate virtual warehouses for ETL and BI — both access the same storage layer without contention
Use a single large warehouse with a prioritized query queue
Enable multi-cluster warehouses and let Snowflake route queries automatically
questions.explanation: Multiple virtual warehouses provide workload isolation. ETL and BI teams each get dedicated compute while sharing the same underlying data, eliminating resource contention.

questions.question: What is the typical uncompressed size range of a Snowflake micro-partition?
options: 1 MB – 10 MB
50 MB – 500 MB
1 GB – 5 GB
100 KB – 1 MB
questions.explanation: Micro-partitions are typically 50–500 MB uncompressed, compressing to roughly 16 MB on disk. Snowflake determines boundaries automatically based on ingestion order.

questions.question: What does a high clustering depth indicate about a table?
options: The table has many clustering keys defined
Micro-partition value ranges for the clustering key heavily overlap — many partitions must be scanned per filtered query, indicating poor clustering effectiveness
The table has a large number of micro-partitions
The table has a high compression ratio
questions.explanation: Clustering depth measures value range overlap across micro-partitions. High depth means key values are scattered, so many partitions must be scanned even for selective queries.

questions.question: What is Automatic Clustering in Snowflake?
options: Snowflake's default behavior of sorting data within each micro-partition
A serverless background service that continuously re-clusters tables with defined clustering keys to maintain low clustering depth
A manual SQL command to reorganize a table's micro-partitions
A feature that automatically selects clustering keys based on query history
questions.explanation: Automatic Clustering is a managed service that monitors and re-clusters tables with defined clustering keys. It uses serverless credits and runs transparently in the background.

questions.question: What is the Search Optimization Service (SOS)?
options: A full-text search engine that automatically indexes all string columns
A table-level option that builds persistent search access paths to accelerate selective point lookup queries and substring searches
A query optimizer feature that rewrites queries for faster execution
A Snowflake-managed cache specifically for lookup tables
questions.explanation: SOS builds auxiliary access path structures for tables to speed up point lookups, equality predicates, and substring searches. Enabled with ALTER TABLE ... ADD SEARCH OPTIMIZATION.

questions.question: What is the Fail-Safe period in Snowflake?
options: The Time Travel window accessible via SQL AT/BEFORE queries
A non-user-accessible 7-day emergency data recovery window maintained by Snowflake after the Time Travel period expires
A 30-day archive retention for compliance purposes
A backup region maintained by Snowflake Support
questions.explanation: Fail-Safe is a 7-day non-user-accessible period after Time Travel expires. Snowflake may use it to recover data in catastrophic failures. Users cannot query or restore from Fail-Safe using SQL.

questions.question: What does 'spilling to remote storage' indicate for a virtual warehouse?
options: The warehouse is saving intermediate results to S3 to free local disk for other operations
A query's intermediate data has exceeded local SSD capacity, overflowing to remote cloud storage — a major performance degradation indicating the warehouse is undersized
The warehouse is automatically migrating cold data to archive storage
Query results larger than 1 GB are being written to a stage instead of returned
questions.explanation: Remote spilling occurs when a query's working set exceeds the warehouse's local SSD. Data overflows to cloud storage (S3/Azure/GCS), which is orders of magnitude slower than local SSD.

questions.question: What is DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS?
options: The number of days Snowflake retains query history
The Time Travel retention period — how many days historical table versions are accessible via AT/BEFORE queries
The Fail-Safe retention period
The result cache retention period
questions.explanation: DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls Time Travel duration. Maximum is 1 day for Standard edition, up to 90 days for Enterprise+. Setting it to 0 disables Time Travel for the object.

questions.question: What is the difference between micro-partition pruning and columnar pruning?
options: They are synonyms for the same optimization technique
Micro-partition pruning skips entire partitions whose column ranges cannot match a filter; columnar pruning reads only the columns referenced by the query within the scanned partitions
Columnar pruning skips null columns; micro-partition pruning skips empty partitions
Columnar pruning applies only to external tables; micro-partition pruning applies to internal tables
questions.explanation: These are complementary optimizations: micro-partition pruning reduces the number of partitions accessed; columnar pruning within those partitions reads only the referenced column files.

questions.question: What is Snowflake Cortex?
options: A Snowflake monitoring dashboard
A suite of serverless AI functions — including LLM text completion, summarization, sentiment analysis, translation, and embedding generation — callable directly from SQL
A feature for training custom deep learning models
A Snowflake Marketplace category for AI data products
questions.explanation: Snowflake Cortex (SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.*) provides serverless AI/ML functions: COMPLETE(), SUMMARIZE(), SENTIMENT(), TRANSLATE(), and EMBED_TEXT_768(). No ML infrastructure to manage — callable from SQL.

questions.question: What is the Snowflake Native App Framework?
options: A mobile SDK for building Snowflake management apps
A framework for building and distributing data applications (SQL, Snowpark, Streamlit) within Snowflake that can be listed on the Snowflake Marketplace and run inside consumer accounts
A REST API framework for Snowflake-backed web services
A CI/CD framework for deploying Snowflake objects
questions.explanation: The Native App Framework packages application logic with data and distributes via the Marketplace. Apps run inside the consumer's Snowflake account using consumer compute — data and logic stay in-platform.

questions.question: What is Snowpark Container Services (SPCS)?
options: A Docker registry for storing Snowpark images
A managed container runtime within Snowflake for running containerized workloads on Snowflake-managed infrastructure with direct access to Snowflake data
A service for packaging Snowpark UDFs as containers
A Kubernetes cluster managed by Snowflake Support
questions.explanation: SPCS allows running full containerized applications — ML model serving, custom APIs, long-running jobs — on Snowflake-managed compute with direct access to Snowflake data and controlled networking.

questions.question: What access control model does Snowflake use?
options: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
Discretionary Access Control (DAC) combined with Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
Rule-Based Access Control
questions.explanation: Snowflake uses DAC (object owners can grant privileges to roles) combined with RBAC (users are assigned roles, roles are granted privileges). Roles can be hierarchically organized for privilege inheritance.

questions.question: Which system role manages billing and account-level settings in Snowflake?
options: SYSADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
ACCOUNTADMIN
ORGADMIN
questions.explanation: ACCOUNTADMIN is the top-level system role, encompassing SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN. It is the only role that can manage billing, account parameters, and resource monitors. It should be tightly restricted.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the SECURITYADMIN role?
options: Managing all database objects
Managing users, roles, and grants/revokes across the account
Creating and managing virtual warehouses
Configuring network policies and authentication methods
questions.explanation: SECURITYADMIN is responsible for user and role management and can grant or revoke any privilege on any object. Business object ownership is SYSADMIN's domain.

questions.question: What is a network policy in Snowflake?
options: A firewall rule in the cloud provider VPC
A Snowflake object that restricts account or user access to a list of allowed IP addresses or CIDR blocks
A policy that limits the number of concurrent connections
A DNS-level routing configuration
questions.explanation: A network policy defines allowed (and optionally blocked) IP ranges. When applied to an account or user, only connections from allowed IPs can authenticate. Created with CREATE NETWORK POLICY.

questions.question: What is the PUBLIC role in Snowflake?
options: A role with full read access to all public tables
A default role automatically granted to every user that can hold minimal shared privileges
A role that represents anonymous (unauthenticated) access
A role that only ACCOUNTADMIN can assign
questions.explanation: Every Snowflake user is automatically granted the PUBLIC role — the lowest-level role in the default hierarchy, used for granting minimal privileges available to all users in the account.

questions.question: What is a dynamic data masking policy in Snowflake?
options: A policy that encrypts column data at rest
A schema-level object that returns masked (obfuscated) values when a column is queried by roles without the unmasked privilege
A column compression algorithm
A policy that hides entire columns from the schema
questions.explanation: A masking policy defines role-based column visibility. Authorized roles see real values; others see masked values (e.g., 'XXXX', NULL, or partial). Real data is never exposed to unauthorized users.

questions.question: How does Snowflake encrypt data at rest by default?
options: Customers must manually enable encryption
All data is automatically encrypted at rest using AES-256 with Snowflake-managed keys
Encryption is optional and incurs additional cost
Only Business Critical accounts encrypt data at rest
questions.explanation: Snowflake encrypts all data at rest with AES-256 automatically and transparently. Customer-managed keys (CMK) are available on Business Critical edition for enhanced control.

questions.question: What does 'GRANT WITH GRANT OPTION' allow?
options: The receiving role can use the privilege and also grant it to other roles
The grant automatically propagates to all child roles in the hierarchy
The grant expires after 24 hours
The receiving role is elevated to ACCOUNTADMIN for that object
questions.explanation: WITH GRANT OPTION allows the grantee role to further delegate that privilege to other roles. Without it, the recipient can use the privilege but cannot pass it on.

questions.question: What is a managed access schema?
options: A schema where all objects are encrypted with a customer-managed key
A schema where only the schema owner (or SECURITYADMIN) can grant privileges on objects within it — preventing object owners from managing their own grants
A schema with automatic row-level security applied to all tables
A schema that is automatically backed up to a secondary region
questions.explanation: In a managed access schema (CREATE SCHEMA ... WITH MANAGED ACCESS), privilege management is centralized. Object owners cannot grant privileges on their own objects — only the schema owner can.

questions.question: What is SAML 2.0 SSO used for in Snowflake?
options: A data encryption protocol
Single Sign-On integration allowing users to authenticate to Snowflake using a corporate identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, etc.)
A multi-region replication feature
A key management protocol for encryption keys
questions.explanation: SAML 2.0 SSO allows organizations to use their existing IdP for Snowflake authentication. Users log in via the IdP — with MFA if configured there — and receive a SAML assertion that Snowflake accepts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'future grants' in Snowflake?
options: Granting privileges that automatically expire in the future
A privilege assignment that automatically applies to all future objects of a specified type created in a schema or database
A grant that is staged for approval by ACCOUNTADMIN
A grant valid only during a future time window
questions.explanation: GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA <s> TO ROLE <r> ensures all tables created in that schema in the future automatically get the specified privilege for that role — reducing ongoing privilege management.

questions.question: What is a row access policy in Snowflake?
options: A policy that hides columns based on user role
A schema-level object applied to a table that filters which rows a querying user or role can see, evaluated at query time
A policy that enforces NOT NULL constraints on rows
An encryption policy applied at the row level
questions.explanation: A Row Access Policy is a function-based filter. When applied to a table, it evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() or CURRENT_USER() for each row — returning TRUE (visible) or FALSE (filtered). All roles including ACCOUNTADMIN are subject to it unless explicitly allowed.

questions.question: What is the SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY view used for?
options: Recording failed login attempts only
A read-only audit log of all user queries showing which tables and columns were accessed — used for compliance auditing and data lineage investigations
Logging warehouse startup and shutdown events
Tracking Data Sharing consumer queries only
questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY records all user queries with base object access details (tables and columns read or written). It is the primary tool for compliance auditing and lineage analysis.

questions.question: What does 'USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL' do in a Snowflake session?
options: Logs out the primary role and switches to all secondary roles
Activates all roles assigned to the user simultaneously — privileges from all assigned roles are combined in the session
Grants all secondary roles ACCOUNTADMIN privileges temporarily
Secondary roles are used only for stored procedure execution
questions.explanation: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL allows the user to access objects owned or accessible under any of their assigned roles simultaneously, without explicitly switching between them.

questions.question: How often does Snowflake automatically rotate its internal encryption keys?
options: Daily
Monthly
Every 30 days
Annually
questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically rotates encryption keys every 30 days. Re-encryption of data under new keys occurs transparently in the background.

questions.question: What is a tag in Snowflake data governance?
options: A SQL comment attached to a column
A schema-level object storing key-value metadata that can be attached to Snowflake objects for classification and governance tracking
A Snowflake configuration file for object documentation
A label that can only be applied to sensitive PII columns
questions.explanation: Tags are first-class Snowflake objects (CREATE TAG). They can be attached to any Snowflake object via ALTER ... SET TAG, powering data classification, lineage, and governance workflows.

questions.question: What is the difference between a column masking policy and a row access policy?
options: Column masking hides entire columns; row access policy hides entire tables
Column masking obfuscates column values for unauthorized roles; row access policy filters which rows a user can see
Column masking is applied automatically; row access policy requires manual configuration
Column masking applies to all roles; row access policy applies only to SECURITYADMIN
questions.explanation: Column masking controls what value is returned for a column (real vs. masked). Row access policies control which rows are visible by filtering the result set based on the querying role or user.

questions.question: How many masking policies can be applied to a single column at one time?
options: Unlimited
Up to 5
Exactly 1
Up to 3
questions.explanation: Only one masking policy can be applied to a column at a time. Applying a second policy without first detaching the existing one results in an error.

questions.question: What Snowflake feature automatically classifies columns as PII using built-in ML classifiers?
options: Automatic tagging via a stream object
Snowflake Data Classification — which suggests system-defined SEMANTIC_CATEGORY and PRIVACY_CATEGORY tags after scanning column names and sample values
An automatic column masking policy detection system
An INFORMATION_SCHEMA sensitivity scanner
questions.explanation: Data Classification uses ML models to analyze column names and sample values, suggesting system-defined tags (e.g., SEMANTIC_CATEGORY: EMAIL, PRIVACY_CATEGORY: IDENTIFIER). Administrators can accept suggestions or run classification programmatically.

questions.question: What is a Data Metric Function (DMF) in Snowflake?
options: A UDF for computing statistical summaries
A function that measures a data quality metric (e.g., null count, freshness) and can be scheduled on tables to track quality over time
A stored procedure for ETL data transformations
A Snowpark function for real-time data validation
questions.explanation: DMFs are SQL functions measuring a quality metric for a table. They can be scheduled and publish results to an event table, enabling ongoing automated data quality monitoring.

questions.question: How can a governance team efficiently find all columns tagged as 'PII' across the entire account?
options: Query INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS and inspect column comments
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TAG_REFERENCES to find all objects with the PII tag applied
Ask ACCOUNTADMIN to run a full table scan across all databases
Use SHOW TAGS in each database individually
questions.explanation: TAG_REFERENCES in ACCOUNT_USAGE (or the INFORMATION_SCHEMA function TAG_REFERENCES()) returns all objects and columns with a specific tag applied — the efficient way to audit PII coverage across the account.

questions.question: What is the Snowflake 'Horizon' governance framework?
options: A new Snowflake storage format released in 2024
Snowflake's unified governance product suite encompassing classification, access control, privacy, lineage, and compliance features
A new region deployment model for Snowflake accounts
A query optimization feature for analytical workloads
questions.explanation: Snowflake Horizon is the branding for Snowflake's end-to-end governance capabilities: data classification, masking policies, row access policies, lineage, data quality metrics, and compliance tooling — all native to the platform.

questions.question: What does applying a masking policy with the FORCE option do?
options: Forces the masking policy to apply even to ACCOUNTADMIN
Atomically replaces any existing masking policy on the column without requiring a prior detach — in a single operation
Forces immediate re-evaluation of all cached query results under the new policy
Forces the policy to propagate to all child tables
questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE ... MODIFY COLUMN ... SET MASKING POLICY ... FORCE replaces an existing masking policy on the column atomically without needing to detach the current one first.

questions.question: Where is a masking policy object stored in Snowflake's object hierarchy?
options: Inside each table it is applied to, as part of the table metadata
As a schema-level object in the schema where it was created with CREATE MASKING POLICY
As an account-level object accessible from all databases
Inside the SNOWFLAKE shared database
questions.explanation: Masking policies are schema-level objects. They can be applied to columns in tables and views across schemas with appropriate privileges, but they live in the schema where CREATE MASKING POLICY was executed.

questions.question: What is the latency for data appearing in SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY?
options: Real-time (less than 1 second)
Up to 45 minutes
24 hours
7 days
questions.explanation: QUERY_HISTORY in ACCOUNT_USAGE has a documented latency of up to 45 minutes. For near-real-time query monitoring, use INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY() or Snowsight's activity view.

questions.question: What is a resource monitor in Snowflake?
options: A Snowflake Support tool for diagnosing performance issues
A Snowflake object that tracks credit usage against a configured quota and can notify administrators or suspend warehouses when thresholds are reached
A performance monitoring dashboard built into Snowsight
A cloud provider billing alert configured externally
questions.explanation: Resource monitors track credit consumption for warehouses or the whole account and trigger configured actions — notify, suspend, or suspend immediately — when usage reaches threshold percentages.

questions.question: What ACCOUNT_USAGE view shows historical virtual warehouse credit consumption per hour?
options: QUERY_HISTORY
WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
STORAGE_USAGE
CREDIT_USAGE_HISTORY
questions.explanation: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY in ACCOUNT_USAGE records hourly credit consumption per virtual warehouse over the past year — the primary view for compute cost trend analysis and chargebacks.

questions.question: What does the ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE view contain?
options: Micro-partition file sizes for all tables in the account
Daily average storage consumption in bytes, broken down by database storage, stage storage, and Fail-Safe storage
Real-time current storage size for all tables
Storage I/O metrics per query
questions.explanation: STORAGE_USAGE captures daily storage snapshots showing average bytes used per category (table storage, stage storage, Fail-Safe). Used for storage cost analysis and capacity forecasting.

questions.question: What does AUTO_SUSPEND primarily help control from a cost perspective?
options: Prevents users from running expensive long-running queries
Eliminates idle warehouse credit consumption by automatically shutting down warehouses after a period of inactivity
Reduces storage costs by purging unused tables
Limits the number of concurrent queries on a warehouse
questions.explanation: Idle warehouses are a major source of unnecessary spend. AUTO_SUSPEND ensures warehouses shut down when not actively processing queries, eliminating idle credit drain.

questions.question: What is a 'budget' in Snowflake's cost management features?
options: A hard credit cap that automatically suspends all warehouses when reached
A Snowflake object that tracks credit spending for a group of objects and alerts when projected spending is on track to exceed a configured monthly threshold
A resource monitor applied at the organization level
A Snowflake Credit alert configured by Snowflake Sales
questions.explanation: Budgets (CREATE BUDGET) define cost-center spending alerts. Unlike resource monitors (which can suspend warehouses), budgets notify administrators when projected monthly spending approaches the configured threshold.

questions.question: Which Snowflake view provides a daily history of all credit consumption broken down by service type?
options: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.CREDIT_CONSUMPTION
questions.explanation: METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily credit totals broken down by service type (virtual warehouse, Snowpipe, Automatic Clustering, etc.) — a complete picture for budgeting and chargeback reporting.

questions.question: Which SQL command loads data from a stage into a Snowflake table?
options: LOAD INTO
INSERT FROM STAGE
COPY INTO <table>
IMPORT TABLE
questions.explanation: COPY INTO <table> is the primary bulk loading command. It reads files from an internal or external stage and inserts rows into the target table, supporting transformation, validation, and error handling.

questions.question: What is the PUT command used for?
options: Uploading data directly into a Snowflake table
Uploading local files to a Snowflake internal stage — example: PUT file:///data/orders.csv @my_stage
Downloading files from an internal stage to a local machine
Sending files to an external stage on S3 or Azure
questions.explanation: PUT uploads local files to internal Snowflake stages. It is available via SnowSQL and Snowflake drivers. Example: PUT file:///data/orders.csv @my_stage.

questions.question: What does the ON_ERROR = CONTINUE option do in COPY INTO?
options: Continues loading even if the target table does not exist
Skips malformed rows and continues loading the remainder of the file, recording errors in load metadata
Retries each errored row 3 times before skipping
Cancels the load but preserves already-loaded rows
questions.explanation: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE causes COPY INTO to skip rows that fail parsing and continue. Errors are recorded in load metadata and retrievable with the VALIDATE() function.

questions.question: What is COPY INTO's default behavior if the same file is submitted for loading twice?
options: All rows are inserted again, creating duplicates
The second load is skipped — Snowflake tracks loaded files for 64 days and ignores already-processed files (FORCE = FALSE)
An error is raised informing the user the file was already loaded
The target table is truncated and fully reloaded
questions.explanation: Snowflake maintains 64 days of file load metadata per stage. By default (FORCE = FALSE), previously processed files are skipped to prevent duplicate loads.

questions.question: What does VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS in COPY INTO do?
options: Validates the stage connection before starting the load
Performs a dry run — scans files and returns errors without inserting any data, used to test file format configurations before a production load
Validates the file format object syntax before creating it
Checks that the target table schema matches the file's column structure
questions.explanation: COPY INTO ... VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS scans files and reports all errors without committing data. The recommended way to verify format configurations before running a production COPY INTO.

questions.question: What is the maximum recommended file size per file for optimal COPY INTO throughput?
options: 1 MB
10–250 MB per compressed file
1 GB per compressed file
There is no recommended limit — Snowflake auto-splits large files
questions.explanation: Snowflake recommends 100–250 MB per compressed file for optimal COPY INTO throughput. Files too small create per-file overhead; files too large reduce parallelism.

questions.question: What does STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE do when loading JSON files?
options: Removes the outermost JSON object wrapper from each row
Strips the outer JSON array, loading each array element as a separate row in the target table
Removes null values from all JSON arrays in the file
Flattens all nested arrays into top-level columns
questions.explanation: When a JSON file is a top-level array ([{...},{...}]), STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE removes the outer brackets and loads each element as a separate row.

questions.question: What does PURGE = TRUE do in a COPY INTO load statement?
options: Deletes rows from the source table after loading them into the destination
Automatically removes successfully loaded files from the stage after the COPY INTO completes
Removes duplicate rows during the load operation
Purges the Time Travel history for the target table
questions.explanation: PURGE = TRUE deletes successfully processed source files from the stage after the load, automating cleanup and preventing accidental re-loading of already-processed files.

questions.question: What is a storage integration in Snowflake external stage definitions?
options: An object that compresses data as it moves between Snowflake and external storage
A reusable object that stores cloud provider credentials (IAM role or service principal) for accessing external cloud storage without embedding credentials in each stage definition
An object that configures network routing for external stage connections
A connector between Snowflake and a cloud data lake platform
questions.explanation: A Storage Integration uses a cloud provider identity (AWS IAM role, Azure service principal, GCP service account) centrally. Multiple external stages reference the same integration, making credential management secure and centralized.

questions.question: What does SKIP_HEADER = 1 do in a CSV file format object?
options: Ignores the first row of each CSV file during COPY INTO — used to skip column header rows
Removes HTTP headers from cloud storage download requests
Skips rows where the first column value is null or empty
Skips loading files with a .header file extension
questions.explanation: SKIP_HEADER = N causes COPY INTO to skip the first N rows of each file — typically used to skip the column header row that should not be loaded as data.

questions.question: What is the COPY_HISTORY view used for?
options: A Snowsight dashboard showing loading activity trends
A table function in INFORMATION_SCHEMA and view in ACCOUNT_USAGE that records all COPY INTO operations, including file names, row counts, errors, and timestamps
A backup log of all COPY INTO SQL statements executed
A general-purpose log of all stage file operations
questions.explanation: COPY_HISTORY is available as INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COPY_HISTORY() (last 14 days, near-real-time) and ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY (up to 1 year with latency). It records file-level load details for auditing and troubleshooting.

questions.question: What does SINGLE = TRUE do when unloading data with COPY INTO @stage?
options: Limits the unload to a single row for testing
Forces all output into a single file instead of Snowflake's default multi-file parallel output
Enables single-threaded processing for small table unloads
Produces a single-column output file
questions.explanation: By default, COPY INTO unload produces multiple parallel files. SINGLE = TRUE forces a single output file — useful when a downstream system requires a single file, at the cost of losing parallelism for large datasets.

questions.question: What is Snowpipe and how does it differ from a scheduled COPY INTO Task?
options: Snowpipe is a batch loader; COPY INTO with a Task is for real-time streaming
Snowpipe is serverless and continuously loads files as they arrive in a stage (typically within minutes); a COPY INTO Task is batch-based and runs on a fixed schedule
Snowpipe requires a virtual warehouse; COPY INTO does not
Snowpipe only supports JSON; COPY INTO supports all file formats
questions.explanation: Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute to detect new files via event notifications or REST API and runs COPY INTO automatically — providing near-real-time ingestion without managing a warehouse or schedule.

questions.question: How does Snowpipe auto-ingest detect new files in an AWS S3 external stage?
options: By polling the S3 bucket on a configurable interval
Via SQS event notifications — S3 publishes ObjectCreated events to a Snowflake-managed SQS queue; Snowpipe listens on the queue and triggers loads for new files
Via AWS Lambda triggers that call the Snowpipe REST API
Via S3 bucket versioning events
questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest on AWS uses S3 → SQS event notifications. S3 publishes ObjectCreated events to an SQS queue managed by Snowflake; Snowpipe listens and triggers loads for newly arrived files.

questions.question: How is Snowpipe compute consumption billed?
options: Per active warehouse hour, the same as virtual warehouses
Per file loaded at a flat rate per file
Using serverless credits at 1.25x the standard warehouse credit rate
Snowpipe is included for free with all Snowflake accounts
questions.explanation: Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute, billed at 1.25× the standard warehouse credit rate per credit consumed. Customers pay only for compute used to process files — no idle cost.

questions.question: What does AUTO_INGEST = TRUE in a CREATE PIPE statement configure?
options: Snowpipe automatically creates the target table if it does not exist
The pipe is configured for event notification-based triggering — requiring a cloud notification integration (SQS, Azure Event Grid, or GCP Pub/Sub) to trigger loads when new files arrive
The pipe starts automatically when the Snowflake account is first created
Auto-ingest enables built-in deduplication of loaded files
questions.explanation: AUTO_INGEST = TRUE configures the pipe to use cloud event notifications. When a new file lands in the source stage, the cloud service sends a notification triggering Snowpipe to load it automatically.

questions.question: What is Snowpipe Streaming (Snowflake Streaming Ingest)?
options: Snowflake's real-time query result streaming to client applications
An SDK-based API for writing rows directly into Snowflake tables from application code with very low latency, without staging files first
A streaming analytics engine built into Snowflake
The Kafka connector's high-throughput operating mode
