questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming (Snowflake Ingest SDK) enables row-level streaming using Java/Python. Applications write rows directly via channels into a table, bypassing the file staging step. Target latency is seconds or sub-second.

questions.question: What does SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS() return?
options: The current error log for a specified pipe
A JSON object describing the pipe's current state: execution status, last ingested file timestamp, pending file count, and notification channel health
A list of all files currently being processed by the pipe
The virtual warehouse being used by the pipe
questions.explanation: SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS('<pipe_name>') returns a JSON document with execution_state, pending_file_count, last_ingested_timestamp, and notification_channel_status — essential for monitoring pipe health.

questions.question: What happens to pre-existing files in a stage when a new Snowpipe pipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE is created?
options: They are loaded immediately upon pipe creation
They are NOT automatically loaded — only files arriving after the event notification is configured trigger auto-ingest; pre-existing files require ALTER PIPE ... REFRESH to queue them
They are loaded in reverse chronological order as a backfill
They are permanently ignored — Snowpipe only processes files created after the pipe object
questions.explanation: Newly created pipes do not backfill pre-existing stage files. Use ALTER PIPE <n> REFRESH to queue them. Only files arriving after the notification is active will load automatically.

questions.question: How does Snowpipe handle duplicate files within its 14-day deduplication window?
options: All rows are inserted again, creating duplicate records
The file is silently skipped — Snowpipe tracks loaded file paths and will not reload the same file path within 14 days
Snowpipe does not track duplicates — this is the developer's responsibility
Duplicates are detected by comparing file checksums at load time
questions.explanation: Snowpipe maintains a 14-day metadata window tracking loaded file paths. Re-submitting the same file path within that window is a no-op, preventing duplicate loads caused by event delivery retries.

questions.question: What Snowpipe credit multiplier applies relative to standard virtual warehouse compute?
options: 0.5x — Snowpipe is half the cost of warehouse compute
1.0x — same cost as warehouse compute
1.25x — Snowpipe serverless credits cost 25% more than warehouse credits
2.0x — Snowpipe costs double warehouse compute
questions.explanation: Snowpipe serverless credits are priced at 1.25× the standard virtual warehouse credit rate. The premium reflects the convenience of fully managed, on-demand serverless compute with zero idle cost.

questions.question: What does ALTER PIPE <name> REFRESH do?
options: Reloads all files that have ever been loaded through the pipe
Queues pre-existing files in the stage's source location for loading — used for initial backfill when a pipe is created for a stage that already contains files
Clears the pipe's error queue and resets the deduplication window
Regenerates the SQS queue ARN for the auto-ingest configuration
questions.explanation: REFRESH scans the pipe's source stage for files not yet loaded and adds them to the ingestion queue. Primarily used for initial backfill at pipe creation or after recovering from a prolonged pause.

questions.question: What is Partner Connect in Snowflake?
options: A marketplace for purchasing Snowflake partner products
A Snowsight feature that simplifies connecting Snowflake to partner tools (dbt, Fivetran, Tableau, etc.) by pre-configuring a service account, warehouse, and database with a few clicks
A secure encrypted tunnel between Snowflake and cloud partner services
A partner support program for Snowflake ISVs and technology partners
questions.explanation: Partner Connect is in Snowsight (Admin → Partner Connect). It creates a dedicated service user, role, warehouse, and database for the selected partner tool and provides one-click setup for the integration.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake external function?
options: A UDF written in a programming language not natively supported by Snowflake
A SQL function that calls an external HTTP endpoint (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) and returns the results — enabling SQL queries to invoke external compute and services
A function stored in a connected external data catalog
A UDF whose code is stored in an external file format object
questions.explanation: External functions allow Snowflake SQL to call external HTTP services via an API Integration. Snowflake sends batched rows to the endpoint and receives results, enabling ML inference, geocoding, or any external service from SQL.

questions.question: How does dbt (data build tool) execute transformations in a Snowflake environment?
options: dbt runs transformations inside its own compute engine, separate from Snowflake
dbt compiles SQL models and executes them directly on Snowflake virtual warehouses via the dbt-snowflake adapter — all compute happens inside Snowflake
dbt extracts data from Snowflake and loads it into a separate transformation database
dbt replaces Snowflake Tasks for scheduled SQL execution and orchestration
questions.explanation: dbt is a SQL transformation framework. It compiles Jinja-templated SQL models and runs them on Snowflake using the Snowflake Python connector under the hood. All compute happens on Snowflake warehouses.

questions.question: What is Snowflake's Git Integration feature?
options: A Snowflake connector for receiving GitHub webhook notifications
A feature that connects Snowflake to a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) and syncs SQL files and Snowpark code into Snowflake for version-controlled deployment and direct execution
A version control system built into the Snowsight worksheet interface
A GitHub Actions workflow for automated Snowflake object deployments
questions.explanation: Git Integration (CREATE GIT REPOSITORY) clones and syncs a Git repo into a Snowflake repository stage. SQL and code files can be executed directly from the repository within Snowflake workflows.

questions.question: What is the Query Profile in Snowflake and how is it accessed?
options: A performance report generated by Snowflake Support on request
A visual execution plan in Snowsight showing each operator node, execution time, bytes processed, and performance insights for a specific query
A stored configuration of query preferences per user or warehouse
A summary of query statistics that can be exported as a JSON file
questions.explanation: The Query Profile (Snowsight → Activity → Query History → select query → Query Profile tab) shows a DAG of operator nodes with timing, row counts, bytes processed, and automated performance warnings.

questions.question: What does 'Bytes Scanned' in the Query Profile indicate?
options: The total bytes returned to the client application
The total compressed bytes read from micro-partitions during the query — lower values indicate more effective micro-partition pruning
The total bytes written to a stage during the query
The total bytes across all tables referenced in the query
questions.explanation: Bytes Scanned shows the total data read from storage. High values relative to table size suggest poor micro-partition pruning. Reducing bytes scanned through better clustering or filter predicates is a key optimization target.

questions.question: What does 'bytes spilled to remote storage' in the Query Profile indicate?
options: The query read data from an external stage location
The query's intermediate data exceeded warehouse memory and local disk capacity, overflowing to S3/Azure/GCS — a major performance degradation indicating the warehouse is undersized
The query result was too large to store in the result cache
The warehouse auto-resumed during the query, spilling temporary state
questions.explanation: Remote spilling means the working set exceeded both warehouse memory and local SSD. Data was written to cloud storage — orders of magnitude slower than local SSD. The warehouse needs to be upsized for this workload.

questions.question: What is query queuing in Snowflake and what causes it?
options: Queries are batched and sent together to reduce overhead
A query waits before execution because all concurrent execution slots on the warehouse are occupied by other queries
A query is delayed due to a result cache miss
A Snowflake pricing model where queries are queued to reduce credit consumption
questions.explanation: Queuing occurs when all warehouse concurrency slots are busy. Signs: high QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME in QUERY_HISTORY. Solutions: increase warehouse size, use multi-cluster warehouses, or enable concurrency scaling.

questions.question: How can a developer confirm a query was served from the result cache?
options: Check if EXECUTION_TIME equals 0 milliseconds in QUERY_HISTORY
QUERY_HISTORY shows BYTES_SCANNED = 0 and near-zero execution time; the Query Profile displays 'Query result was reused'
Result cache queries have a different format for their QUERY_ID
Snowsight displays a 'Cached' badge on every row of query history served from cache
questions.explanation: Result cache hits appear in QUERY_HISTORY with bytes_scanned = 0 and near-zero execution time. The Query Profile explicitly states 'Query result was reused', confirming no warehouse compute was consumed.

questions.question: What does QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME in QUERY_HISTORY represent?
options: Total query execution time including all processing steps
Time spent waiting in the warehouse queue because all concurrent execution slots were occupied — high values indicate the warehouse is oversubscribed
Time spent waiting for the warehouse to finish provisioning before the query could start
Time blocked by a concurrent transaction holding a lock
questions.explanation: QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME records how long a query waited for an available execution slot. High values indicate the warehouse needs more concurrency capacity — a larger size, multi-cluster, or concurrency scaling.

questions.question: What is the best first diagnostic step when a previously fast query is now running slowly?
options: Restart the virtual warehouse to clear any stale state
Compare the slow query's Query Profile against a historical fast run in QUERY_HISTORY — look for changes in bytes_scanned, spilling, pruning efficiency, or compile time to identify the root cause
Immediately increase the warehouse size and re-run the query
Rewrite the query from scratch using a different approach
questions.explanation: The diagnostic workflow: pull both query IDs from QUERY_HISTORY, compare Query Profiles, look for increased bytes_scanned (more data/less pruning?), spilling (data growth exceeding warehouse?), or long compile time (schema changes?).

questions.question: Which Snowflake feature speeds up highly selective point lookups like WHERE email = 'user@example.com' on large tables?
options: A clustering key defined on the email column
The Search Optimization Service (SOS) configured with EQUALITY search method on the email column
A materialized view pre-filtering by email domain
The result cache, which caches the first lookup for reuse
questions.explanation: SOS is optimized for highly selective point lookups on high-cardinality columns. It builds access paths enabling Snowflake to skip nearly all micro-partitions for exact-value lookups — far more effective than clustering for this pattern.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of defining a clustering key on a large Snowflake table?
options: Enforces uniqueness on the clustered columns
Improves micro-partition pruning for queries that filter on the clustering key columns, reducing bytes scanned and execution time
Reduces the number of columns stored per micro-partition
Enables more aggressive compression for the clustered columns
questions.explanation: A clustering key sorts data so micro-partitions contain non-overlapping value ranges for the clustered columns. Queries filtering on those columns can skip most partitions, dramatically reducing scan volume.

questions.question: What is predicate pushdown in Snowflake query optimization?
options: Pushing filter conditions to the client application before sending the query
The optimizer applying WHERE clause filters as early as possible in the execution plan — ideally at the scan level — to reduce the amount of data processed in subsequent operators
Storing frequently used filter predicates as named objects for reuse
Transferring filter logic to a UDF for parallel execution across warehouse nodes
questions.explanation: Predicate pushdown applies filter conditions early in the execution plan, preventing Snowflake from reading and processing data that will ultimately be filtered out. This is the foundation of micro-partition pruning.

questions.question: Why is UNION ALL more efficient than UNION in Snowflake when duplicates are known not to exist?
options: UNION ALL is never more efficient — UNION is always the correct choice for safety
UNION ALL avoids the deduplication sort/hash step that UNION requires, reducing both compute and memory usage — with no correctness impact when duplicates cannot exist
UNION ALL is only safe when combining STRING columns
UNION ALL requires all columns to have identical data types; UNION handles type mismatches
questions.explanation: UNION performs a deduplication step (sort or hash), consuming extra compute and memory. UNION ALL simply concatenates results. When duplicates are impossible or irrelevant, UNION ALL is always more efficient.

questions.question: What is a lateral join in Snowflake and what is it most commonly used for?
options: A JOIN between tables in different schemas within the same account
A join allowing a table-valued function on the right side to reference columns from the left-side table — most commonly used with LATERAL FLATTEN() to explode JSON arrays from each row
A left outer join with a lateral subquery in the WHERE clause
A join that processes rows left-to-right sequentially rather than in parallel
questions.explanation: A LATERAL join allows the right-side expression to reference left-side table columns. LATERAL FLATTEN(src.events) explodes the events array for each row, correlating the explosion with the current row's context data.

questions.question: What is the QUALIFY clause in Snowflake SQL used for?
options: Qualifying column names with table aliases to avoid ambiguity
Filtering rows based on window function results — equivalent to wrapping a query in a subquery and filtering on the window function output, but more readable and often more efficient
Applying a data quality validation check to all result rows
Determining whether a query is eligible to use the result cache
questions.explanation: QUALIFY is a Snowflake/BigQuery extension that filters window function output without requiring a subquery. For example: SELECT ..., RANK() OVER (...) AS r FROM t QUALIFY r = 1 is cleaner than an equivalent outer subquery.

questions.question: What is 'transitive predicate closure' optimization in Snowflake?
options: Expanding boolean expressions for faster partial evaluation
When a filter is applied to a join key, Snowflake infers the same filter should apply to the joined table's matching key — enabling pruning on both sides of the join without explicit filtering
Closing open transactions before beginning query compilation
Using transitive metadata to locate partitions through the storage layer
questions.explanation: If you write WHERE a.id = b.id AND a.id = 5, Snowflake infers b.id = 5. This enables micro-partition pruning on the b table even without an explicit filter, dramatically reducing the scan on the un-filtered join side.

questions.question: What is 'subquery flattening' (unnesting) performed by Snowflake's optimizer?
options: Removing CTEs and inlining them as subqueries for faster parsing
Converting correlated subqueries (IN, EXISTS, scalar subqueries) into equivalent JOIN operations that can be parallelized more efficiently across warehouse nodes
Caching subquery results in the metadata layer for reuse
Rewriting correlated scalar subqueries as window functions
questions.explanation: Subquery unnesting converts correlated subqueries into parallel JOINs. Joins are far more efficiently distributed across MPP nodes than correlated row-by-row subquery evaluation — a high-impact optimizer transformation.

questions.question: What is incremental refresh for dynamic tables and materialized views?
options: Dropping and recreating the entire object on each refresh cycle
Only recomputing the portions of the result that have changed since the last refresh — based on new or changed source rows — rather than rerunning the full query from scratch
Refreshing the object in small time windows to spread the compute load
A feature available only on Enterprise edition and above
questions.explanation: Incremental refresh detects changes in source tables and only recomputes affected output rows. This is far more efficient than full re-computation for large tables with a small percentage of row changes per refresh.

questions.question: What is CLUSTER BY (TRUNC(date_col, 'MONTH')) better at than CLUSTER BY (date_col) for month-range filters?
options: TRUNC expressions are not valid in Snowflake clustering key definitions

TRUNC groups all dates in a month to the same key value, colocating monthly data in the same micro-partitions and improving pruning for month-level range filters; date-level clustering creates finer but more numerous overlapping partition boundaries
Month-level clustering increases clustering depth, degrading performance
TRUNC on the clustering key disables Automatic Clustering
questions.explanation: Expression clustering keys reduce key cardinality. TRUNC(date, 'MONTH') means all dates in a month share the same clustering value, grouping them into shared partitions. Month-range queries prune far more effectively against month-level clusters.

questions.question: When is it more appropriate to use a dynamic table instead of a stream + task pipeline?
options: When you need sub-second data freshness in the target table

When the transformation is a SQL query and you want declarative, automatically refreshed materialization with configurable lag — instead of manually managing stream offsets, task schedules, and error handling
When you need to process data arriving from Kafka directly
When the source table has more than 1 billion rows
questions.explanation: Dynamic tables are the declarative alternative to stream+task. You define the SQL and target lag; Snowflake handles incremental refresh, scheduling, and retries. Stream+task is more flexible but requires explicit orchestration.

questions.question: How many distinct cache layers does Snowflake have?
options: 1 — the result cache only
2 — result cache and warehouse local disk cache
3 — result cache (cloud services), local disk/SSD cache (warehouse nodes), and remote cloud storage as the permanent fallback
4 — result cache, memory cache, local disk cache, and remote disk cache
questions.explanation: Snowflake has three layers: (1) Result cache in cloud services — exact query results for 24 h; (2) Local SSD cache on each warehouse node — recently accessed micro-partitions; (3) Remote cloud storage — the permanent data layer and fallback.

questions.question: What conditions must be met for the result cache to serve a query?
options: The exact same SQL text must be submitted by any user
The exact SQL text must match, the referenced objects must not have changed since the result was cached, and the querying role context must be the same
The same SQL text must be submitted by the same user within 1 hour
Any syntactically equivalent query from any user within 24 hours will hit the cache
questions.explanation: Result cache conditions: (1) Exact SQL text match, (2) Referenced table data unchanged, (3) Same role context, (4) Same relevant session parameters. Any user meeting these criteria in the same account can benefit.

questions.question: Does serving a result cache hit require a virtual warehouse to be running?
options: Yes — the warehouse must be running to retrieve cached results
No — result cache hits are served entirely by the cloud services layer, consuming zero warehouse compute credits
Yes — a small X-Small warehouse is used to serve cache retrievals
No, but the warehouse must be provisioned (not suspended) before the cache can be queried
questions.explanation: Result cache is stored and served by cloud services. A cache hit consumes zero warehouse credits — the result is returned directly from cloud services, making it the highest-leverage optimization in Snowflake.

questions.question: What session parameter disables the result cache for a session?
options: RESULT_CACHE_ENABLED = FALSE
USE_CACHED_RESULT = FALSE — disabling result cache for the session while leaving the local disk cache fully active
DISABLE_RESULT_CACHE at the warehouse level
Result cache cannot be disabled for individual sessions
questions.explanation: USE_CACHED_RESULT = FALSE (session parameter) forces re-execution of every query, useful for benchmarking or testing whether data has changed. The local SSD cache on warehouse nodes remains active.

questions.question: What eviction policy does Snowflake use for the local disk (SSD) cache on warehouse nodes?
options: Random eviction — any cached micro-partition may be evicted when space is needed
Least Recently Used (LRU) — least recently accessed micro-partitions are evicted first to make room for newly read partitions
First In First Out (FIFO) — the oldest loaded partitions are evicted first
Most Recently Used (MRU) — recently used partitions are evicted to preserve older cached data
questions.explanation: Snowflake uses LRU eviction for the local SSD cache. Partitions not accessed recently are evicted to make room for newly read partitions, keeping frequently accessed (hot) partitions resident longer.

questions.question: What is 'cache thrashing' in the context of Snowflake warehouses?
options: The result cache filling up and evicting entries faster than they can be repopulated
Frequent warehouse suspend and resume cycles that repeatedly clear the local SSD cache, resulting in consistently cold cache performance for workloads that depend on warm local cache
Multiple warehouses competing for access to the same micro-partitions simultaneously
A condition where too many small files are cached simultaneously, causing capacity issues
questions.explanation: Cache thrashing occurs when a warehouse is suspended and resumed so frequently that the local cache never warms up. Workloads benefiting from local cache should have AUTO_SUSPEND configured to allow cache persistence between related query runs.

questions.question: What is Snowpark and what programming languages does it support?
options: A Snowflake-specific SQL dialect for advanced transformations
A developer framework for writing data pipelines and ML workloads in Python, Java, or Scala using DataFrame APIs, executed directly on Snowflake warehouse compute
A low-code drag-and-drop data transformation tool built into Snowsight
A Snowflake add-on module for running R analytics workloads
questions.explanation: Snowpark lets developers write code-first transformations. DataFrames in Python, Java, or Scala are compiled into SQL and executed on Snowflake warehouses, keeping data in-platform without movement.

questions.question: What is MERGE INTO used for in Snowflake data transformation pipelines?
options: Permanently combining two tables into a single merged table
Performing an upsert (UPDATE existing rows + INSERT new rows) and optional DELETE in a single atomic statement based on a match condition — the core pattern for incremental data loads
Merging micro-partitions together to improve table clustering depth
Joining two CDC streams into a single combined change stream
questions.explanation: MERGE INTO is the standard upsert command: WHEN MATCHED → UPDATE; WHEN NOT MATCHED → INSERT; WHEN MATCHED AND <condition> → DELETE. It is the primary pattern for incremental pipeline updates.

questions.question: What is the FLATTEN function in Snowflake used for?
options: Collapsing a nested CTE into a single table scan for performance
Laterally exploding a VARIANT/ARRAY/OBJECT column into individual rows — one row per array element or key-value pair — enabling relational processing of semi-structured data
Flattening a poorly clustered table to improve micro-partition pruning
Merging micro-partitions together to reduce the overall partition count
questions.explanation: FLATTEN is a Snowflake table function that explodes ARRAY or OBJECT VARIANT values into tabular rows. Used with LATERAL to correlate the explosion with the current row's context.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Snowflake stored procedure and a UDF?
options: Stored procedures run faster than UDFs for all operation types
Stored procedures support procedural logic (loops, conditionals, error handling) and can execute DDL and DML; UDFs can only return scalar or table values and cannot run DDL
Stored procedures are compiled code; UDFs are always interpreted
Stored procedures run in parallel across warehouse nodes; UDFs are always single-threaded
questions.explanation: Stored procedures support full procedural code (Python, JavaScript, Java, Scala) including control flow, error handling, and DDL/DML execution. UDFs are pure functions that return values but cannot create tables or run transactions.

questions.question: What is a dynamic table's 'target lag' parameter?
options: The replication lag to a secondary account for disaster recovery
The configurable maximum age of data in the dynamic table — how stale the output can be before Snowflake triggers an automatic incremental refresh
The time interval between scheduled Task executions in a DAG
The delay before a masking policy takes effect after being applied to a column
questions.explanation: Target lag defines the maximum acceptable staleness. Snowflake monitors source tables and triggers incremental refresh when the dynamic table's data age approaches the configured lag (e.g., '1 minute', '1 hour').

questions.question: What is a DAG of Tasks in Snowflake?
options: Multiple independent Tasks that all run in parallel at the same scheduled time
A hierarchical pipeline of Tasks where child Tasks depend on the completion of parent Tasks — defined using the AFTER <parent_task> clause in CREATE TASK — enabling complex multi-step workflows
A directed acyclic graph of SQL statements within a single stored procedure
A Snowflake Streams topology showing the flow of change data through the pipeline
questions.explanation: Task DAGs: a root task orchestrates the pipeline; child tasks specify AFTER <parent>. The root task, when started, triggers the chain in dependency order — child tasks execute after their parents complete successfully.

questions.question: What is CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) commonly used for in transformation pipelines?
options: Creating a view backed by a SELECT query for repeated use
Materializing the result of a SELECT query into a new permanent, transient, or temporary table — used to persist expensive intermediate transformations for reuse or downstream consumption
Creating an external table from a SELECT query that reads external files
Creating a clone of the SELECT query's source table with the same data
questions.explanation: CTAS materializes a query result as a physical table. In transformation pipelines, CTAS persists expensive intermediate results, avoiding recomputation, and creates denormalized analytical tables from complex joins and aggregations.

questions.question: What are Snowflake Notebooks?
options: Jupyter notebooks hosted externally and connected to Snowflake via JDBC
Interactive cell-based development environments built into Snowsight supporting Python, SQL, and Markdown cells — running on Snowflake compute with direct access to Snowflake data
Read-only documentation tools embedded in Snowsight for sharing analysis
Scheduled notebooks that replace Tasks for running data pipeline transformations
questions.explanation: Snowflake Notebooks are native interactive notebooks in Snowsight. They support Python, SQL, and Markdown cells with direct access to Snowflake data. Python cells run on Snowpark (warehouse or container services) without data leaving Snowflake.

questions.question: What is the maximum Time Travel retention period available on Enterprise edition?
options: 1 day
7 days
30 days
90 days
questions.explanation: Enterprise edition supports up to 90 days of Time Travel (DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 90) for permanent tables. Standard edition supports up to 1 day.

questions.question: After the Time Travel retention period expires, where does the data go?
options: It is permanently and immediately deleted from Snowflake storage
It moves into the 7-day Fail-Safe period before being permanently deleted
It is archived to Glacier-equivalent cold storage at reduced cost
It is converted to a read-only snapshot table for historical access
questions.explanation: After Time Travel expires, data enters the 7-day Fail-Safe — a non-user-accessible emergency recovery window. After Fail-Safe expires, data is permanently deleted from Snowflake storage.

questions.question: Which SQL syntax queries a table as it existed at a specific past timestamp?
options: SELECT * FROM t AT TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01 00:00:00'
SELECT * FROM t AT(TIMESTAMP => '2024-01-01 00:00:00'::TIMESTAMP_TZ)
SELECT * FROM t WHERE _LOAD_TIME = '2024-01-01'
SELECT * FROM t VERSION AS OF '2024-01-01'
questions.explanation: Time Travel queries use the AT clause: SELECT * FROM my_table AT(TIMESTAMP => '2024-06-01 12:00:00'::TIMESTAMP_TZ). The BEFORE clause queries the state just before a specific event or statement executed.

questions.question: What does UNDROP do in Snowflake?
options: Restores an accidentally deleted row to its original table
Recovers a dropped table, schema, or database within the Time Travel retention period — restoring both the object definition and all data
Undoes the last DML statement on a table using Time Travel
Recovers a file that was deleted from a Snowflake internal stage
questions.explanation: UNDROP TABLE <n> (or UNDROP SCHEMA / DATABASE) restores a recently dropped object within its Time Travel window. Both metadata and data micro-partitions are restored as they were at the moment of the DROP.

questions.question: What is database replication in Snowflake and what does it protect against?
options: Copying tables to a different schema within the same account for testing
Replicating databases to secondary Snowflake accounts in different regions or cloud providers, providing business continuity against region-level failures and enabling cross-region analytics
Replicating Snowflake data to an external S3 bucket for archival
Creating a Zero-Copy Clone of a database in a different Snowflake account
questions.explanation: Database replication continuously replicates database objects to secondary accounts. On failover, a secondary can be promoted to primary, supporting disaster recovery across regions and cloud providers.

questions.question: What is Zero-Copy Clone's storage impact immediately after creation?
options: Storage equal to the full source table is charged immediately
Zero additional storage at creation — the clone shares source micro-partitions via copy-on-write metadata; new storage is only charged as either the source or clone is modified
50% of source storage is charged as a metadata management fee
Storage is free for the first 7 days then billed at the full rate
questions.explanation: Zero-Copy Clone uses copy-on-write. At creation, the clone shares all source micro-partitions at no additional cost. Storage costs arise only when either the source or clone is modified, creating divergent new micro-partitions.

questions.question: What is Snowflake Secure Data Sharing?
options: A feature that copies data to consumer accounts for their own analysis
A mechanism allowing providers to share live, read-only access to data with consumer Snowflake accounts — without copying data — using metadata pointers to the provider's existing storage
An encrypted data transfer service between two Snowflake accounts
A marketplace for purchasing curated datasets from third-party providers
questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing creates metadata share objects pointing consumers to the provider's underlying micro-partitions. No data movement occurs; consumers query the provider's live data through a virtual read-only database in their own account.

questions.question: What types of objects can be included in a Snowflake share?
options: Tables only
Tables, external tables, secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs
Any object including warehouses and stages
Only schemas and entire databases
questions.explanation: Shares can include tables, external tables, secure views, secure materialized views, dynamic tables, and secure UDFs. Non-secure views cannot be shared as they expose provider SQL logic. Warehouses and stages cannot be shared.

questions.question: Does data sharing consume storage in the consumer's account?
options: Yes — data is physically copied to the consumer's storage at the time the share is accessed
No — consumers query through metadata pointers; no data is copied and no storage is charged to the consumer for the shared data
Yes — at a discounted rate of 50% of normal storage pricing
No, unless the consumer runs CREATE TABLE AS SELECT from the shared data
questions.explanation: No data is copied during sharing. Consumers access a virtual read-only database backed by the provider's micro-partitions. Only warehouse compute credits are charged to the consumer for running queries against the shared data.

questions.question: What is the latency of data changes in a Snowflake share?
options: Data in the share is refreshed on a daily batch schedule
Near-real-time — consumers see data changes typically within seconds of the provider committing a transaction, since the share uses live metadata pointers
Data is synchronized hourly between provider and consumer accounts
45-minute latency, matching the ACCOUNT_USAGE view latency
questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing uses metadata pointers to provider live storage. Data changes committed by the provider are visible to consumers with near-zero latency — typically within seconds.

questions.question: Why must views included in a Snowflake share be defined as secure views?
options: For performance reasons — secure views are optimized for cross-account access

Regular views expose their SQL definition to consumers, potentially revealing provider business logic. Secure views hide the definition, showing only query results — protecting provider intellectual property
Secure views are required by Snowflake's compliance certifications for all sharing
Regular views cannot be used across account boundaries for technical reasons
questions.explanation: A SECURE VIEW hides its SQL from consumers — SHOW CREATE VIEW returns only column names, not the SELECT logic. This protects sensitive business rules and prevents consumers from inferring data not intended for sharing.

questions.question: What is a Snowflake data clean room?
options: A compliance-certified environment where all data is encrypted with customer-managed keys

A privacy-preserving collaboration environment where two parties can run joint SQL analyses on their combined data without either party seeing the other's raw underlying records — built on Secure Data Sharing and secure views
A dedicated Snowflake account environment for GDPR compliance workloads
A staging area where data is cleansed and validated before loading into production
questions.explanation: A data clean room uses Secure Data Sharing and carefully designed secure views with aggregation constraints to allow two parties (e.g., advertiser and publisher) to compute joint metrics without exposing individual-level records.

questions.question: What technology powers the Snowflake Marketplace?
options: A Snowflake-managed data lake with nightly refresh
Snowflake Secure Data Sharing — each listing is backed by a share giving consumers instant, live, zero-copy access to provider data
A proprietary Snowflake CDN for global data distribution
AWS Data Exchange with Snowflake integration
questions.explanation: The Marketplace is Secure Data Sharing at scale. Each listing is backed by a share. When consumers get a listing, they receive a share giving instant live access to the provider's data without any data movement or copying.

questions.question: What are the two main pricing models for Snowflake Marketplace listings?
options: Pay-per-query and annual subscription only
Free (provider shares at no charge) and commercial (provider charges for data access, billed through Snowflake's commerce platform)
Free and premium (premium requires Business Critical edition)
Credits-based and dollar-based billing models
questions.explanation: Marketplace listings are either free (public data, marketing datasets) or commercially priced. Commercial listings are billed through Snowflake's commerce platform — consumers pay providers via the Marketplace.

questions.question: What is a Native App on the Snowflake Marketplace?
options: A mobile application for managing a Snowflake account from a smartphone
A data application built with the Snowflake Native App Framework (SQL, Snowpark, Streamlit) that runs entirely within the consumer's Snowflake account — combining data and business application logic
A Snowflake-hosted web application served to users outside Snowflake
A BI dashboard product published to the Marketplace for purchase
questions.explanation: Native Apps package application logic (stored procedures, UDFs, Streamlit UI) and optionally data, distributed via the Marketplace. They run in the consumer's Snowflake account using consumer compute, keeping everything in-platform.

questions.question: A compliance analyst wants to automatically detect and label sensitive customer data across databases. Which feature directly enables this?
options: Data classification
Object tagging
Dynamic Data Masking
Row access policy
questions.explanation: Data classification automatically detects and labels sensitive data across databases using built-in classification capabilities.

questions.question: An Administrator is reviewing credit consumption and notices that several resource monitors have been created recently. How do resource monitors affect credit consumption?
options: Resource monitors consume credits while tracking warehouse usage.
A fixed monthly charge applies per account when resource monitors are enabled.
Resource monitors track compute usage associated with warehouses while operating at no additional cost.
Credits are charged for each alert or suspension action triggered by a resource monitor.
questions.explanation: Resource monitors do not consume credits. They only track compute usage and can trigger alerts or suspend warehouses based on thresholds.

questions.question: An Administrator is looking to evaluate their account against security recommendations. Which feature should they use?
options: Trust Center
The ACCOUNT_USAGE schema
Access History
Network Policies
questions.explanation: Trust Center provides a centralized view to evaluate security posture and recommendations for a Snowflake account.

questions.question: Which access control framework should be used if the desired outcome is to allow the role that owns an object to grant access to that object?
options: Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
User-Based Access Control (UBAC)
Role hierarchy and privilege inheritance
questions.explanation: DAC allows object owners to grant privileges on objects they own to other roles.

questions.question: A team needs to securely host, manage, and execute custom, non-SQL code written in Python and Java for data processing and transformation workflows. Which Snowflake feature will meet these requirements while ensuring performance meets demands?
options: External function
Snowpark
Stored procedure
Cortex Complete
questions.explanation: Snowpark enables developers to write and execute code in languages like Python and Java directly within Snowflake, ensuring performance and scalability.
